TL;DR: The paper addresses the problem of learning high-level causal models from low-level measurements, providing theoretical foundations and a practical objective.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To shift the focus from hypothesis-testing to a learning-based paradigm for high-level causal models, tackling the gap in deriving structured abstractions directly from data.

Method: The authors leverage low-rank causal discovery hypotheses, demonstrating the formation of causal abstractions, establishing identifiability results, and proposing an objective for learning high-level structural causal models (SCM).

Result: The study concludes that low-rank graphs can induce latents forming causal abstractions, provides theoretical identifiability guarantees of these latents, and introduces a practical approach to learn high-level models.

Conclusion: The paper advances the field of causal abstraction by presenting methods to derive high-level SCMs directly from low-level data, addressing limitations in current expert-proposed models and enabling a data-driven approach.

Abstract: Causal abstractions formalize when a high-level structural causal model (SCM) captures the interventional behavior of a lower-level SCM. Existing applications of this notion largely follow a hypothesis-testing paradigm: an expert proposes a candidate high-level model and then evaluates if the low-level system implements it. We study the complementary problem of learning a high-level model directly from low-level measurements. Our contributions leverage hypotheses from low-rank causal discovery, and can be summarized as follows: (1) we show that observations generated by a low-rank graph induce latents that form a causal abstraction, (2) we provide identifiability results about these latents, and (3) we propose a practical objective to learn this high-level SCM.

</details>


### [289] [IHBench: Evaluating Post-Interruption Recovery in Voice Agents with Structured Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19595)
*Ahmad Salimi,Wentao Ma,Yuzhi Tang,Dongming Shen,Mu Li,Alex Smola*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: IHBench evaluates how voice agents recover from user interruptions during state-machine-driven workflows, scoring task fulfillment and recovery quality. Closed-weight models outperform open-weight ones in robustness and recovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Voice agents in structured workflows often encounter user interruptions. Existing benchmarks focus on interruption timing but overlook post-interruption recovery, which impacts user experience and task success.

Method: The researchers developed IHBench, a benchmark that assesses recovery from six controlled interruption types across workflows in 10 domains. They scored recovery on task fulfillment and recovery quality across 27 audio-language model configurations.

Result: Closed-weight models outperformed open-weight ones, demonstrating more robust interruption handling. They showed slower performance degradation in longer conversations and no modality gap between audio and text-based interactions.

Conclusion: Recovery from interruptions is a distinct and critical capability for voice agents. Closed-weight models demonstrate better task fulfillment and recovery quality, suggesting they are better suited for structured workflows.

Abstract: Voice agents deployed in structured workflows (customer service, healthcare scheduling, account management) must handle frequent user interruptions while maintaining progress through multi-step procedures. Existing benchmarks for speech-capable models focus on the timing of interruptions: barge-in detection, endpointing, and turn-taking dynamics. They leave unmeasured what happens after the interruption: does the agent resume the workflow at the correct step? Does it address the user's interjection? Does it avoid re-delivering content the user already heard?
  We introduce IHBench (Interruption Handling Benchmark), a benchmark that evaluates post-interruption recovery in voice agents executing state-machine-driven workflows across 10 enterprise domains. Six interruption types are injected at controlled points mid-utterance, with per-interruption evaluation rubrics generated alongside the data. Each interruption is scored on two axes: task fulfillment and recovery quality.
  We evaluate 27 audio-language model configurations from OpenAI, Google, and the open-weight community. Models vary widely, and recovery quality depends strongly on the interruption type. Across our experiments, closed-weight models are consistently more robust to interruptions than open-weight ones: they win far more often on task fulfillment, degrade roughly 3.3x more slowly as conversations grow longer, and show no audio-versus-text modality gap, whereas the open-weight models lose ground on all three. A human study validates the LLM judge against human annotators, and a cross-benchmark analysis against AudioMultiChallenge indicates that recovery quality is a largely distinct capability axis.

</details>


### [290] [Comparing Linear Probes with Mahalanobis Cosine Similarity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19603)
*Zhuofan Josh Ying,Peter Hase,Nikolaus Kriegeskorte*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates Mahalanobis cosine similarity (MCS) as a refined metric for comparing linear probes in interpretability research, showing strong predictive correlation with OOD AUROC.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Linear probes are widely used in interpretability studies but require more task-relevant metrics beyond standard cosine similarity for deeper analysis.

Method: The authors extend empirical findings across various domains and prove mathematically that MCS is closely tied to OOD AUROC through a shared dependence on probe signal-to-noise ratio, verifying theoretical predictions with empirical tests.

Result: They demonstrate that MCS predicts OOD AUROC with near-perfect linear correlation (R^2 = 0.98) across contexts, except in specific scenarios where their theory forecasts failure.

Conclusion: MCS provides a robust and theory-backed alternative to cosine similarity, advancing the analysis of linear probes in interpretability experiments.

Abstract: Linear probes are widely used in interpretability research and often compared by cosine similarity. The Mahalanobis cosine similarity (MCS) between two directions, which reweights the inner product by test data covariance, is a natural task-aware refinement. Ying et al. (2026) report that a probe's MCS to a reference probe trained on the out-of-distribution (OOD) data near-perfectly linearly predicts the probe's OOD AUROC (R^2 = 0.98). Here, we extend this empirical finding across models, layers, and concept domains, and prove this general phenomenon in closed form: For balanced classes whose projections are Gaussian, OOD AUROC and MCS to the reference probe are linear because both are sigmoid-shaped functions of the probe's signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) on the test data. The theory also predicts when this linearity fails, which we verify empirically. MCS offers a theoretically grounded and empirically effective alternative to Euclidean cosine similarity for comparing linear probes.

</details>


### [291] [Latent Confounded Causal Discovery via Lie Bracket Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19610)
*Sridhar Mahadevan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces two causal discovery algorithms, BRIDGE and SKFM, grounded in Kan-Do-Calculus. These methods leverage geometric and information-theoretic approaches to identify latent structures and address latent confounders in causal inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation of this paper is to advance causal inference methods by addressing the challenge of latent confounders, using theoretical tools from category theory and information geometry to bridge observational and interventional regimes.

Method: The paper presents two novel algorithms: BRIDGE, which uses residuals from non-closing causal vector fields to identify latent variables, and SKFM, which employs spectral decomposition of intervention fields to identify latent curvature and streamline discovery.

Result: Experiments demonstrate that the proposed algorithms can discover causal models in the presence of latent confounders, significantly reducing the search space of possible directed acyclic graphs (DAGs).

Conclusion: This work provides a novel paradigm for causal discovery, leveraging the geometry of intervention-induced flows to uncover latent structure, and enhances the capability of algorithms to handle latent confounding in causal analysis.

Abstract: Recent work on Kan-Do-Calculus (KDC) has established that the boundary between passive observation and active intervention in causal inference is a category-theoretic bi-adjunction, with interventions modeled by left Kan extensions and conditioning by right Kan extensions. This paper introduces two causal discovery algorithms under latent confounding, building on the information-geometric and categorical consequences of KDC. In smooth statistical settings, Radon-Nikodym derivatives between observational and interventional measures induce local causal vector fields; failures of these fields to close under Lie brackets become computable Frobenius residuals, which we interpret as witnesses of failed visible integrability and possible latent or unmodeled structure. Our first algorithm, BRIDGE (Bracket Residuals for Interventional Discovery and Geometric Estimation), combines an interventional density or Radon-Nikodym-ratio engine with a geometric screen that proposes a high-recall family of admissible arrows, identifies non-closing visible pairs as latent-obstruction candidates, and passes the reduced family to downstream score-based or differentiable discovery routines. The second algorithmic contribution, Spectral Kan-Do Flow Matching (SKFM), learns amortized intervention fields and factors latent curvature spectrally, exposing the direct Lie-space endpoint toward which BRIDGE points. A detailed set of experiments show that both algorithms are capable of discovering causal models with latent confounders while collapsing the super-exponential space of possible DAGs by many orders of magnitude. This paper introduces a new paradigm in causal discovery, where latent structure is inferred directly from the geometry of intervention-induced flows.

</details>


### [292] [SEAGAN: domain-Specific and Edge-Aware Graph Attention Network for Dynamic Plant Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19623)
*Antriksh Srivastava,Soumyashree Kar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Graph neural networks (GNNs) are used to classify biochemical limitation states from A-Ci curves in plant physiology, improving classification near transition regions using edge-aware attention and specific node features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the challenge of reliably identifying biochemical limitation states in plant physiological A-Ci curves, which is vital for estimating photosynthetic parameters but remains uncertain.

Method: The method involves representing A-Ci curve points as nodes in graph-based models using domain-specific connectivity (kNN and ASG), employing SEAGAN architecture with edge-aware graph attention and weighted cross-entropy loss.

Result: SEAGAN achieves an F1-score of 0.857 and 88.2% accuracy on a synthetic dataset, outperforming conventional baselines and benchmarks in limitation-state classification.

Conclusion: Graph-based models, particularly with edge-aware attention mechanisms, enhance the analysis of biochemical limitation states in photosynthesis by leveraging structured connectivity and node features.

Abstract: Graph neural networks (GNNs) provide a flexible framework for learning from scientific data linked through physical, biological, or functional relationships. One promising domain is plant physiology, where measured responses often arise from multiple interacting processes whose exact separation remains difficult even with manual intervention. In plant physiology, a key example is the A-Ci curve, which relates net CO2 assimilation rate (Anet) to leaf intercellular CO2 concentration (Ci) and is used to estimate photosynthetic parameters in leaf and crop-canopy models. However, reliable estimation requires identifying the active biochemical limitation state at each curve point, which remains a major source of uncertainty. Here, we formulate limitation-state identification along A-Ci curves as a graph-based node classification problem, with curve points as nodes. Domain-specific graph representations are created using distance-based k-nearest-neighbor (kNN) and auxiliary-signal-guided (ASG) connectivity, with edge attributes encoding pairwise relations. The framework was evaluated against conventional learning baselines, graph-based architectures, and an automated fitting-based benchmark. Results on a large synthetic dataset with known ground-truth limitation states show that graph-based models improve classification, particularly near biochemical transition regions. The best-performing configuration, SEAGAN (domain-Specific and Edge-Aware Graph Attention Network for Dynamic Plant Processes), integrates process-aware node features, edge attributes, kNN connectivity, and graph attention with weighted cross-entropy loss, achieving an F1-score of 0.857 and an accuracy of 0.882. The results show that representing A-Ci curves as graphs improves biochemical limitation-state analysis, with edge-aware attention over local kNN neighborhoods providing the most effective strategy.

</details>


### [293] [MassSpecGym in the Wild: Uncovering and Correcting Evaluation Pitfalls in AI-Driven Molecule Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19624)
*Hongxuan Liu,Roman Bushuiev,Ivy Lightheart,Mrunali Manjrekar,Anton Bushuiev,Magdalena Lederbauer,Filip Jozefov,Yinkai Wang,Soha Hassoun,Josef Sivic,James Taylor,Runzhong Wang,David Healey,Tomáš Pluskal,Connor W. Coley*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies evaluation flaws in machine learning benchmarks for tandem mass spectrometry, introducing MassSpecGym v1.5 to address these issues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Highlight the need for trustworthy benchmarking in MS/MS-based molecule discovery and address issues compromising reliability.

Method: Conducted a review of 26 papers using MassSpecGym benchmarks, quantified impact of issues through experiments, and refined benchmarking standards.

Result: Uncovered 17 papers exhibiting evaluation problems, isolated three classes of major failures, and released MassSpecGym v1.5 addressing these failures.

Conclusion: Benchmarking issues in MS/MS must be resolved to ensure meaningful and reliable evaluations. MassSpecGym v1.5 offers an improved framework.

Abstract: Reliable benchmarking is critical for developing machine learning models for tandem mass spectrometry (MS/MS) based molecule discovery. Subtle issues in experimental design and model evaluation procedures can degrade the trustworthiness of such benchmarks and lead to erroneous conclusions. We conduct a thorough review of model evaluation issues in the recent MS/MS machine learning literature, using the standard MassSpecGym benchmark suite as a case study to illustrate the impact of these issues. We find evaluation issues in at least 17 of 26 papers reporting MassSpecGym benchmark results in the first year of its adoption. We isolate three classes of failures: (i) data leakage, (ii) shortcut learning, and (iii) implementation bugs and metric divergence. Through extensive experimentation and code replication, we quantify the impact of these issues and show how they corrupt the evaluation standards MassSpecGym was designed to enforce. We distill our findings into recommendations generalizable to MS/MS challenges, benchmarks, and custom evaluation setups. We also release MassSpecGym v1.5, an implementation of our recommendations in the MassSpecGym benchmarking suite which addresses the failure modes identified in this audit. MassSpecGym v1.5 is publicly available at https://github.com/pluskal-lab/MassSpecGym.

</details>


### [294] [Hard or Just Unreached? Diagnosing the Sampling Blind Spot in Math-Reasoning Difficulty Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19636)
*Luca Zhou,Sajel Shah,Emanuele Rodolà,Roberto Dessì*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper critiques reliance on standard pass@k metrics for evaluating math and science benchmarks, showing that seemingly unsolvable examples can be addressed with deterministic approaches and targeted activation perturbations. This suggests limitations in pass@k for capturing underlying model capacity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks for math and science reasoning often rely on pass@k metrics, which measure a model's ability to generate correct outputs within a certain number of attempts. This metric is central to many system evaluations and training improvements, but it may fail to capture nuanced difficulties in certain subsets of data.

Method: The authors tested eight free-form math datasets using multiple open-weight models. They applied greedy decoding alongside five activation grafting perturbations to examine their effectiveness on examples deemed unsolvable within the usual pass@k framework. They also analyzed mechanistic distinctness across cells using Jaccard similarity metrics.

Result: They found that 10.3-22.9% of the hardest examples (unsolved after multiple sampling tries) were solvable using deterministic decoding regimes combined with residual perturbations. Recovery improved with additional compute budgets, and mechanistic distinctions between perturbations were verified.

Conclusion: The pass@k signal has limitations, missing examples that are solvable with alternative decoding strategies. The results underline the importance of considering structural features within the residual stream and expand the diagnostic toolkit for evaluating latent model capabilities.

Abstract: Math and science reasoning benchmarks rely on pass@k, the fraction of sampled chains that reach gold, as the canonical per-example difficulty signal. The same signal drives RL with verifiable rewards, math data curation, synthetic curricula, and verifier training. We show this proxy has a persistent blind spot on its hardest stratum: on the eight free-form math cells we test (GSM8K and MATH across four open-weight models), 10.3-22.9% of the examples that no sampling seed solves in six tries are instead solved at matched compute by a six-chain deterministic regime. These are greedy decoding plus five cheap residual-stream perturbations applied via activation grafting, while greedy alone solves at most 6% on these math cells. Recovery scales with the additional budget, across perturbations whose mechanistic distinctness we verify across all twelve cells (cross-kind fix-set Jaccard <= 0.47 in every setup). Activation grafting is used as an intervention on internal representations, not a decoding method; we use it purely as a diagnostic and diversification tool, and our recovered items show that the pass@k= 0 % stratum is structurally identifiable in the residual stream rather than that the unmodified model reaches them under ordinary inference.

</details>


### [295] [Convex training of Lipschitz-regularized shallow neural networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19652)
*Chao Yin,Antoine Lesage-Landry*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new training method for shallow neural networks to improve their robustness against adversarial attacks using a convex restriction of a Lipschitz-regularized training task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance the adversarial robustness of shallow neural networks, addressing the challenges posed by non-convex optimization through a globally optimal convex approximation.

Method: A training procedure leveraging a convex restriction of a non-convex Lipschitz-regularized program, used as a post-processing step on pre-trained networks to guarantee improved or equal outcomes.

Result: The proposed convex training procedure leads to networks with lower objective values on the Lipschitz-regularized program and greater robustness against adversarial attacks on certain datasets.

Conclusion: The convex training method is effective for improving accuracy and robustness of shallow neural networks under adversarial scenarios, offering advantages over existing techniques.

Abstract: In this work, we introduce a training procedure for shallow neural networks that promotes robustness against adversarial attacks. We solve a non-convex Lipschitz-regularized training program by introducing a convex restriction that can be efficiently solved to global optimality. Our approach can be employed as a post-processing step by taking a pre-trained network as an initial solution to then solving the convex program whose optimal network is guaranteed to be no worse than the initial one. We illustrate the improvements of our training procedure with experiments using real world datasets for regression tasks under an adversarial setting. We show numerically that solving our proposed convex program yields networks with lower objective values on the Lipschitz-regularized program compared to existing methods. Additionally, we show that on certain datasets, networks obtained using our convex training program are both more accurate and robust with respect to adversarial attacks.

</details>


### [296] [LOKI: Memory-Free Null-Space Constrained Lifelong Knowledge Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19679)
*Masih Eskandar,Miquel Sirera Perelló,Stratis Ioannidis,Jennifer Dy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LOKI is a new method for lifelong knowledge editing in models, using dynamic layer selection and null-space gradient projection to improve efficiency and reduce catastrophic forgetting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for lifelong model updates tend to rigidly change fixed layers and rely on prior knowledge access, resulting in limited flexibility and potential performance issues.

Method: LOKI employs dynamic layer selection using Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion and gradient updates projected on the model's null-space, avoiding reliance on past knowledge.

Result: Experimentation shows LOKI outperforms existing methods, providing up to 14% improvement in average accuracy.

Conclusion: LOKI offers a more adaptive and effective approach for updating language models sequentially over time, without requiring pre-access to past knowledge.

Abstract: Lifelong knowledge editing aims to efficiently and sequentially update language models over time, as new knowledge becomes available or when the model makes mistakes, while preserving acceptable performance on past knowledge. One unresolved challenge is that existing methods modify a fixed set of layers for all new knowledge samples, reducing flexibility and increasing catastrophic forgetting. Another is requiring access to previous knowledge and extensive pre-processing to obtain data statistics. To address these challenges, we introduce LOKI, a novel approach that uses dynamic layer selection based on the Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion and projects gradient updates onto the null-space of the model weights, bypassing the requirement for previous knowledge access. We show that LOKI achieves superior performance to existing approaches across a wide variety of experiments, achieving up to a 14\% improvement in average accuracy.

</details>


### [297] [Multi-Granular Attention-Driven Reinforcement Learning Framework for Web Intelligent Enhancement Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19690)
*Navin Chhibber,Deepak Singh,Anokh Kishore,Nikita Chawla,K. Anguraj*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces MGAR-WIES, a system that uses graph modeling, attention mechanisms, and reinforcement learning to improve intelligent web services, achieving an accuracy of 80%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional machine learning and reinforcement learning models struggle with semantic understanding, adaptability, and scalability in evolving web environments.

Method: The authors created MGAR-WIES which preprocesses heterogeneous web data, transforms it into a dynamic semantic graph enhanced by attention mechanisms, and uses an adaptive multi-agent reinforcement learning strategy to optimize web actions.

Result: MGAR-WIES outperformed existing methods and achieved 80% accuracy.

Conclusion: Integrating semantic graph modeling, attention mechanisms, and reinforcement learning improves adaptability, scalability, and performance of intelligent web systems.

Abstract: From the past few years, web intelligent enhancement systems increasingly rely on heterogeneous and dynamic web data to deliver personalized, context-aware services. However, traditional machine learning, deep learning, and reinforcement learning models often struggle with semantic understanding, adaptability, and scalability in continuously evolving web environments. In this research, a Multi-Granular Attention-based Reinforcement Web Intelligent Enhancement System (MGAR-WIES) is proposed to address the challenges by integrating semantic graph modeling, attention mechanisms, and adaptive reinforcement learning. Initially, heterogeneous web data comprising structured, semi-structured and unstructured sources are collected and preprocessed for generating unified feature representations. These representations are transformed into a dynamic semantic graph, where entities and their relationships are modeled by using graph embeddings enhanced by attention mechanisms for capturing both local relevance and global contextual dependencies. Subsequently, an adaptive multi-agent reinforcement learning strategy leverages the attention-aware semantic states to optimize personalized web actions like content recommendation, navigation optimization, and service adaptation. Finally, the continuous online feedback is further integrated to update graph representations and learning policies in real time by ensuring sustained adaptability and performance. The proposed MGAR-WIES acheived better results in terms of accuracy (80%) when compared with existing approaches.

</details>


### [298] [Efficiently Representing Algorithms With Chain-of-Thought Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19697)
*Yanhong Li,Anej Svete,Ashish Sabharwal,William Merrill*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether chain-of-thought (CoT) transformers can efficiently simulate Word RAM algorithms and concludes they can do so with poly-logarithmic overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While Turing machines provide a theoretical foundation for computation, practical and efficient algorithm analysis is typically done using the Word RAM model. The motivation is to determine if CoT transformers, which are gaining popularity, can match the computational efficiency of Word RAM algorithms.

Method: The authors analyze CoT transformers in three settings: finite-precision transformers with specific attention mechanisms, continuous CoT (vector-based reasoning), and a hybrid architecture combining transformers with linear RNNs. They measure simulation efficiency compared to the Word RAM model.

Result: The study shows that CoT transformers can simulate any Word RAM algorithm with only poly-logarithmic overhead, and this efficiency improves to log-square or logarithmic overhead depending on the Word RAM's instruction set.

Conclusion: CoT transformers are not only capable of simulating Turing machines but are also efficient in simulating Word RAM algorithms, making them practical for higher-level algorithmic tasks with minimal computational overhead.

Abstract: The increasing popularity of \emph{reasoning} models -- language models that output a series of reasoning or thought tokens before producing an answer -- is justified, in part, by theoretical results showing that chain-of-thought (CoT) transformers can simulate Turing machines, and thus perform arbitrary computation. However, the Turing machine, while suitable for complexity-theoretic analysis, is not convenient, intuitive, or efficient for discussing algorithms. Algorithms are typically designed and analyzed at a higher level of abstraction, captured by the \emph{Word RAM} model with random-access memory and unit-cost operations on $\bigO(\log n)$-bit words. As a result, Word RAM algorithms can be substantially more efficient than their Turing machine counterparts, raising the question: \emph{Can CoT transformers efficiently simulate Word RAM algorithms?} For instance, can they sort $n$ items in $\bigO(n \log n)$ steps or run Dijkstra's algorithm in $\bigO(E + V \log V)$ steps? We answer affirmatively, up to poly-logarithmic overhead. We first establish this for finite-precision transformers with poly-logarithmic width and rightmost unique hard attention, then strengthen the result to two more practical settings with finite width and log-precision: \emph{continuous} CoT, where reasoning takes the form of vectors rather than tokens, and a \emph{hybrid} architecture in which transformer layers sit atop a recurrent (linear RNN) layer. In all three cases, we find that CoT \emph{can} efficiently simulate any Word RAM algorithm with only a poly-logarithmic overhead in $n$. This overhead reduces to log-square when the Word RAM has a ``flat'' instruction set, and only logarithmic for multiplication-free flat instructions -- in stark contrast to known CoT simulations of Turing machines, which require quadratic overhead over Word RAM.

</details>


### [299] [Efficient Neural Network Model Selection for Few-Class Application Datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19712)
*Bryan Bo Cao,Abhinav Sharma,Lawrence O'Gorman,Michael Coss,Shubham Jain*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a method for efficient model selection in real-world applications with fewer than ten classes by leveraging dataset properties and a new 'few-class distinctiveness' metric.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap in efficient model selection for real-world scenarios with few-class datasets, often overlooked compared to traditional high-class neural network benchmarks.

Method: They propose a classification difficulty metric based on data properties, enabling faster (6 to 29 times) model and dataset evaluation compared to traditional repeated training/testing.

Result: The study shows models scaled below YOLOv5-nano are up to 42% smaller while maintaining accuracy, demonstrating usefulness in mobile robots and IoT settings.

Conclusion: The proposed method offers significant efficiency gains for resource-limited applications without performance compromise, enhancing practical utility in scenarios with few classes.

Abstract: While much effort has focused on developing and benchmarking high-performance neural networks, less attention has been given to how dataset properties, known to practitioners, can guide efficient model selection. Neural models are typically evaluated on datasets with thousands of classes, yet many real-world applications involve fewer than ten. To address this understudied but common setting, we develop a measure of classification difficulty based on data-side properties and show how it enables more efficient model selection for few-class datasets, where traditional approaches are less effective. We term this phenomenon "few-class distinctiveness". Our metric allows comparison of models and datasets 6 to 29$\times$ faster than repeated training and testing. Leveraging this insight, we extend scaled model families below the smallest published models, achieving greater efficiency at similar accuracy, for example models up to 42% smaller than YOLOv5-nano for a mobile robot task. Targeting resource-constrained applications, we demonstrate few-class model selection across mobile robot, drone, and IoT scenarios, highlighting practical gains in efficiency without sacrificing performance.

</details>


### [300] [OnDeFog: Online Decision Transformer under Frame Dropping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19721)
*Daiki Yotsufuji,Kenta Nishihara,Shoma Shimizu,Kento Uchida,Shinichi Shirakawa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces OnDeFog, an online reinforcement learning method to handle performance issues caused by frame dropping, outperforming existing offline (DeFog) and online (ODT) methods in challenging scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the impact of frame dropping in real-world reinforcement learning scenarios by creating a robust method that performs well in situations with high frame drops and low-reward data.

Method: Integrate mechanisms from DeFog with the Online Decision Transformer (ODT) to develop OnDeFog, which learns policies through real-time interaction with the environment.

Result: Experimental results show OnDeFog outperforms ODT in environments with high frame dropping rates and exceeds DeFog in datasets with low-reward occurrences.

Conclusion: OnDeFog provides a more reliable and effective approach for handling frame dropping in reinforcement learning than existing offline or online methods.

Abstract: In challenging real-world reinforcement learning applications, communication delays or sensor failures often cause frame dropping, in which the agent cannot receive the dropped states and associated rewards. To address the performance degradation caused by frame dropping, the Decision Transformer under Random Frame Dropping (DeFog) was developed by incorporating additional mechanisms into the decision transformer to tackle frame dropping. Although DeFog can mitigate performance degradation in frame-dropping environments, since DeFog is an offline learning method, it struggles to effectively generalize to novel states not adequately represented in the training dataset. In this study, we propose OnDeFog, which integrates the mechanisms in DeFog with the online decision transformer (ODT), an online reinforcement learning method that learns policies through direct environmental interaction. Comprehensive experimental evaluation demonstrates that our proposed OnDeFog achieves superior performance compared to ODT in environments characterized by high dropping frame rate and outperforms DeFog on datasets containing a large amount of low-reward data.

</details>


### [301] [Federated Bilevel Performative Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19734)
*Liangxin Qian,Chang Liu,Xuanyu Cao,Jun Zhao,Kwok-Yan Lam*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Federated Bilevel Performative Prediction, considering client-specific, decision-dependent data shifts, and presents two federated methods to solve it with theoretical and experimental validations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the challenge of performativity in federated bilevel optimization, where client data distributions shift based on decisions, rendering existing fixed distribution assumptions inadequate.

Method: The paper formalizes the concept of Federated Bilevel Performatively Stable (FBPS) points and proposes two methods: FBi-RRM, with linear convergence under a contraction condition, and FBi-SGD, a stochastic method with convergence guarantees under diminishing step sizes.

Result: The proposed methods improved stability thresholds, better meta-generalization, and demonstrated efficacy in strategic regression, meta strategic classification, and CNN-based neural network classification.

Conclusion: The introduced methods address performative challenges effectively, enhancing federated optimization's adaptability to decision-dependent distribution shifts while validating their practicality across diverse experiments.

Abstract: Federated bilevel optimization is widely used for nested learning problems across distributed clients, such as federated hyperparameter tuning and meta-learning under privacy and communication constraints. Most existing formulations assume fixed client data distributions, which can be violated by performativity, where deployed decisions reshape client behavior and data collection, inducing client-specific, decision-dependent distribution shift. We study federated bilevel performative prediction, where both upper-level (UL) and lower-level (LL) objectives are evaluated under client-dependent, decision-dependent distributions. We formalize the federated bilevel performatively stable (FBPS) point under a decoupled-risk perspective and provide sufficient conditions for its existence and uniqueness. We then develop two federated methods to compute the FBPS solution: FBi-RRM, which converges linearly under a contraction condition, and FBi-SGD, a communication-efficient stochastic method based on federated hypergradient estimation with convergence guarantees under diminishing step sizes when sensitivities are sufficiently small. Experiments on strategic regression and meta strategic classification validate the predicted stability thresholds and demonstrate improved meta-generalization over non-performative baselines, and CNN-based classification further demonstrates the practical effectiveness of the proposed methods in nonconvex neural network settings.

</details>


### [302] [Manifold Bandits: Bayesian Curriculum Learning over the Latent Geometry of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19750)
*Darrien McKenzie,Nicklas Hansen,Xiaolong Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes Bayesian Manifold Curriculum (BMC), a novel framework for task sampling in RL for large language models, emphasizing structure-awareness over traditional difficulty-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to improve training efficiency and outcomes in reinforcement learning for LLMs by addressing the limitations of existing problem-sampling methods, which overlook the structured and interrelated nature of task space.

Method: The proposed method, BMC, organizes problems into a hierarchical task tree and utilizes Bayesian learning to guide problem sampling, treating task selection as a manifold-structured bandit problem.

Result: Empirical studies reveal that different sampling strategies impact trade-offs between productivity, diversity, and utility, demonstrating that focusing solely on difficulty-based sampling is insufficient for optimal performance.

Conclusion: Incorporating the structure and type-awareness of problems within the sampling process is crucial for enhancing downstream performance in RL for LLMs.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) is a central approach for improving reasoning capabilities in large language models (LLMs), where training efficiency depends critically on how problems are sampled during optimization. Existing adaptive curriculum learning methods typically prioritize prompts of intermediate difficulty, treating problem selection as a standard bandit problem with independent arms and overlooking the structured, heterogeneous nature of the task space. In this work, we frame problem sampling as a manifold-structured bandit problem with endogenous non-stationarity: problems are related through the model's latent representation space, and sampling decisions can steer how learning signals evolve across that space. To operationalize this perspective, we introduce Bayesian Manifold Curriculum (BMC), a structure-aware framework that organizes problems into a hierarchical task tree and applies Bayesian learning to guide sampling. Empirically, we find that different sampling strategies induce non-trivial tradeoffs between productivity (learning signal), diversity (coverage of the task manifold), and utility (evaluation relevance). These results show that prioritizing difficulty alone is insufficient for strong downstream performance, highlighting the importance of incorporating structure and type-awareness into problem sampling.

</details>


### [303] [Learning universal approximations for partial differential equations with Physics-Informed Broad Learning System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19754)
*Zhiwen Yu,Derong Yang,Liujian Zhang,Kaixiang Yang,Peilin Zhan,Jianmin Lv,Jane You,C. L. Philip Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces the Physics-Informed Broad Learning System (PIBLS), which is a fast and efficient approach for solving PDEs with significantly better accuracy compared to PINNs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional numerical solvers for PDEs are computationally costly, and while PINNs address this by being mesh-free, they face issues like slow convergence and instability. This paper aims to provide a solution that overcomes these shortcomings.

Method: The paper proposes PIBLS, a framework that avoids backpropagation and reformulates PDE solving as a direct least-squares optimization. It includes an improved algorithm for efficiently solving nonlinear PDEs and provides a mathematical proof of its universal approximation capability.

Result: The proposed PIBLS framework demonstrated one to three orders of magnitude faster performance than PINNs, alongside significantly improved accuracy in solving both linear and nonlinear PDEs.

Conclusion: PIBLS offers a computationally efficient and accurate alternative for solving PDEs, suitable for tasks like real-time simulation and design optimization, marking a significant advance in scientific machine learning.

Abstract: Partial differential equations (PDEs) play a central role in modeling complex physical, biological, and engineering systems. While traditional numerical solvers are robust, they often incur prohibitive computational costs due to mesh dependencies, whereas recent Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) offer a mesh-free alternative but frequently suffer from slow convergence and optimization instability. To bridge this gap, this article proposes the Physics-Informed Broad Learning System (PIBLS), a novel backpropagation-free framework that reformulates PDE solving as a direct least-squares optimization. We improved an algorithm within this framework to handle nonlinear PDEs efficiently and provide a rigorous mathematical proof establishing the universal approximation property of PIBLS for these equations. Experiments on linear and nonlinear PDEs demonstrate that PIBLS is one to three orders of magnitude faster than conventional PINNs while achieving significantly higher solution accuracy. This framework provides a computationally efficient paradigm for scientific machine learning, offering a practical, high-speed alternative for real-time simulation and design optimization tasks.

</details>


### [304] [An Information Theoretic Framework for Graph Novelty Generation via Latent Mixture Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19770)
*Itsuki Nakagawa,Kenji Yamanishi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a method for generating novel and structurally consistent graph data using an information-theoretic approach.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need to produce distinct and reliable graph data arises in scenarios where innovation and structural consistency are paramount while avoiding duplication of existing patterns.

Method: The method uses a latent space representation, finite mixture modeling for latent distributions, and samples generation under conditions of novelty and reliability based on description length metrics.

Result: Theoretical analysis confirms low probabilities of misclassification for non-novel or unreliable samples, and experiments on various datasets validate the approach's efficacy for controlled novelty generation.

Conclusion: The framework enables principled graph novelty creation with demonstrable risk quantification, maintaining structural integrity.

Abstract: We propose an information-theoretic framework for graph novelty generation, which aims to generate data that are distinct from existing patterns while preserving global structural consistency. Our approach embeds data into a latent space, models the latent distribution using finite mixture models, and generates novel samples by imposing explicit novelty and reliability conditions formulated in terms of description length. Specifically, novelty is enforced by requiring generated samples to be poorly explained by all existing mixture components, while reliability constrains their impact on the overall mixture structure under the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle. We provide a theoretical analysis showing that, with appropriate threshold choices, the probabilities of misclassifying non-novel or unreliable samples converge to zero with explicit rates. Experiments on synthetic and benchmark graph datasets demonstrate that the proposed method enables principled novelty generation with quantifiable risk.

</details>


### [305] [Flow Map Denoisers: Traversing the Distortion-Perception Plane for Inverse Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19802)
*Nicolas Zilberstein,Morteza Mardani,Santiago Segarra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper addresses the tradeoff in image restoration between minimizing error and maximizing perceptual quality, proposing a novel approach with flow map models that balance this tradeoff.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge in image restoration where minimizing error compromises perceptual quality and enhancing perceptual quality reduces fidelity, and to enable a method that bypasses the limitations of current single operating points or cumbersome tuning.

Method: A flow map model, an extension of flow matching for few-step sampling, is introduced, with a lookahead parameter t used to continuously navigate the distortion-perception frontier.

Result: The approach precisely recovers the optimal distortion-perception frontier for Gaussian targets and shows similar promising behavior for natural images. Plug-and-Play solvers extend this mechanism to general inverse problems. Extensive experiments demonstrate it matches or outperforms specialized baselines.

Conclusion: Flow map models offer a flexible, effective solution for image restoration by balancing fidelity and perceptual quality, presenting a streamlined, powerful alternative to specialized or overly complex existing methods.

Abstract: Image restoration faces a fundamental tradeoff: methods that minimize error produce blurry reconstructions, while those that maximize perceptual quality yield sharp but less faithful images. Existing approaches either commit to a single operating point on this distortion perception (DP) frontier or require paired-data supervision, auxiliary models, or hyperparameter tuning of the sampler to access different points. We show that flow map models, a recent extension of flow matching for few-step sampling that learns an average field, implicitly define a one-parameter family of denoisers that continuously spans the DP frontier. The lookahead parameter t acts as a control knob between the MMSE and perceptual regimes. For Gaussian targets, we prove that varying t exactly recovers the optimal DP frontier; for natural images, we observe similar behavior empirically. Within a Plug-and-Play solver, the same mechanism extends to general inverse problems, where it controls a tradeoff between perceptual alignment and data consistency. Despite the lack of exact optimality guarantees in this setting, a single trained flow map spans the DP tradeoff, matching or exceeding specialized baselines at both extremes. Extensive experiments on CelebA ($128\times 128$) and AFHQ ($256\times 256$) across several linear and nonlinear inverse tasks validate our findings.

</details>


### [306] [Uncertainty-Aware Reward Modeling for Stable RLHF](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19818)
*Licheng Pan,Haocheng Yang,Haoxuan Li,Yichen Sun,Yunsheng Lu,Shijian Wang,Lei Shen,Yuan Lu,Zhixuan Chu,Hao Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Uncertainty-Aware Reward Modeling (UARM) to address reward hacking issues in RLHF pipelines. UARM improves reward model reliability and downstream alignment quality, validated by experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the vulnerabilities arising from unreliable predictions in reward models and uniform reward treatment in policy optimization during RLHF, which can lead to reward hacking.

Method: The authors propose UARM, utilizing quantile-based conformal prediction for calibrated uncertainty in reward models and heteroscedastic variance decomposition for adaptive weighting during GRPO advantage computation.

Result: Experiments on HelpSteer, UltraFeedback, and PKU-SafeRLHF show UARM enhances reward model calibration, reduces reward hacking, and improves alignment quality compared to baselines.

Conclusion: UARM addresses fundamental issues in RLHF pipelines, mitigating reward hacking and improving the reliability and alignment quality of reward models.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) aligns large language models by training reward models on preference data and optimizing policies to maximize predicted rewards. However, this pipeline faces two fundamental challenges: (1) reward models cannot signal when their predictions are unreliable, since they usually act as deterministic point estimators; and (2) modern group-based policy optimization can amplify unreliable reward signals, as exemplified by GRPO's uniform treatment of rewards during advantage computation. As policies explore increasingly diverse responses, these two limitations create a critical vulnerability: unreliable reward estimates may be granted disproportionate influence, triggering severe reward hacking. We propose Uncertainty-Aware Reward Modeling (UARM), which equips reward models with calibrated uncertainty via quantile-based conformal prediction and reweights GRPO advantages through heteroscedastic variance decomposition. Experiments across HelpSteer, UltraFeedback, and PKU-SafeRLHF demonstrate that UARM significantly improves reward model calibration, reduces reward hacking, and enhances downstream alignment quality compared to standard GRPO and uncertainty-agnostic baselines.

</details>


### [307] [Enhancing Graph Neural Networks Using Proximity Graphs for Dust Source Emission Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19825)
*Maryam Sanisales,Zahed Rahmati,Ali Darvishi Boloorani,Ali Vefghi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper explores how Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) combined with proximity graphs can better predict dust source emissions compared to traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the shortcomings of traditional methods in predicting dust source emissions, which fail to capture complex spatiotemporal dynamics of dust storms.

Method: The authors propose using proximity graphs (e.g., Delaunay triangulation, Gabriel graph, k-Nearest Neighbor graph, Yao graph) as input for GNNs (e.g., GraphSAGE, Graph Convolutional Networks, Graph Attention Networks) to model spatial and temporal relationships more effectively.

Result: GNNs combined with proximity graphs outperform GNNs with random graphs and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) models in predicting dust source emissions.

Conclusion: Integrating proximity graphs with GNNs demonstrates robust and accurate results in tackling dust source emission forecasting challenges.

Abstract: Accurate prediction of dust source emissions is critical for mitigating the significant environmental and health hazards posed by dust storms. Traditional forecasting methods often struggle to capture the complex spatiotemporal dynamics of these phenomena.
  In this paper, we demonstrate that proximity graphs enable Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to effectively model the intricate spatial and temporal relationships between data points. Specifically, we use proximity graphs--such as Delaunay triangulation, Gabriel graph, k-Nearest Neighbor graph, and Yao graph--as the input for GNNs (including GraphSAGE, Graph Convolutional Networks, and Graph Attention Networks) to perform message passing.
  Our approach highlights the effectiveness of integrating proximity graphs with GNNs for robust and accurate dust source forecasting. To emphasize the importance of proximity graph representations, we compare our method against GNNs using random graphs for message passing. The results show that GNNs with proximity graphs significantly outperform those with random graphs and are also far superior to Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model in dust source emission forecasting.

</details>


### [308] [When, Where, and How: Adaptive Binning for Tabular Self-Supervised Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19827)
*Daehwan Kim,Haejun Chung,Ikbeom Jang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper addresses challenges in deep learning for medical tabular data and introduces Adaptive Binning, a self-supervised learning method tailored for such data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning for medical tabular data is underexplored due to the high cost of obtaining reliable labels, even though structured data is readily available. Existing methods lack adaptivity in handling diverse feature types, motivating an improved approach.

Method: The authors propose "Adaptive Binning," which uses a training-adaptive discretization pretext. This method employs a feature-wise coarse-to-fine curriculum, detects plateaus, and refines discretization adaptively, incorporating ordinal supervision for numerical features and categorical reconstruction.

Result: The method was evaluated using public medical tabular datasets and showed consistent performance improvements without requiring dataset-specific tuning. Additionally, the authors introduced a benchmark for medical tabular SSL with standardized protocols.

Conclusion: Adaptive Binning demonstrates significant potential for improving deep learning applications in medical tabular data, offering better representation learning and flexibility compared to existing methods. The development of a new benchmark further supports reproducibility and progress in the field.

Abstract: Medical tabular data are ubiquitous in clinical research, but deep learning for tables remains underexplored because reliable labels often require costly expert adjudication, even though structured clinical variables are routinely available in tabular form. Self-supervised learning can leverage these unlabeled tables, and recent binning-based pretexts offer a promising inductive bias, but existing objectives fix a single global quantile discretization and apply feature-agnostic supervision. We propose Adaptive Binning, a training-adaptive discretization pretext for tabular SSL that couples discretization to learning through a feature-wise coarse-to-fine curriculum. Motivated by the spectral bias of neural networks and the principles of curriculum learning, our method progressively refines discretization per feature upon plateau detection and selects representation-aware splits to jointly improve value-space concentration and representation-space coherence. A heterogeneity-aware objective unifies categorical reconstruction with ordinal supervision for numerical features, and experiments on public medical tabular datasets under unified evaluation protocols show consistent gains for linear probing and fine-tuning without dataset-specific discretization tuning. We further introduce a medical tabular SSL benchmark with standardized protocols to support reproducible progress in this underexplored domain. Our code is available at https://github.com/labhai/Adaptive-Binning.

</details>


### [309] [Neural Additive and Basis Models with Feature Selection and Interactions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19850)
*Yasutoshi Kishimoto,Kota Yamanishi,Takuya Matsuda,Shinichi Shirakawa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study addresses the computational inefficiency of Neural Additive Models (NAMs) and Neural Basis Models (NBMs) in high-dimensional settings by introducing a feature selection mechanism, enhancing both interpretability and computational efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the computational efficiency and flexibility of NAMs and NBMs, especially in handling feature interactions and high-dimensional datasets.

Method: The paper proposes incorporating a feature selection layer into NAM and NBM, where selection weights are updated during training to optimize resource usage and allow two-input NN usage efficiently in high-dimensional contexts.

Result: The modified NAM and NBM models demonstrate reduced computational costs, smaller model sizes, and the ability to consider feature interactions in high-dimensional datasets, with performance comparable or superior to state-of-the-art GAMs.

Conclusion: Enhancing NAMs and NBMs with feature selection makes them more computationally efficient and interpretable while retaining competitive predictive performance.

Abstract: Deep neural networks (DNNs) exhibit attractive performance in various fields but often suffer from low interpretability. The neural additive model (NAM) and its variant called the neural basis model (NBM) use neural networks (NNs) as nonlinear shape functions in generalized additive models (GAMs). Both models are highly interpretable and exhibit good performance and flexibility for NN training. NAM and NBM can provide and visualize the contribution of each feature to the prediction owing to GAM-based architectures. However, when using two-input NNs to consider feature interactions or when applying them to high-dimensional datasets, training NAM and NBM becomes intractable due to the increase in the computational resources required. This paper proposes incorporating the feature selection mechanism into NAM and NBM to resolve computational bottlenecks. We introduce the feature selection layer in both models and update the selection weights during training. Our method is simple and can reduce computational costs and model sizes compared to vanilla NAM and NBM. In addition, it enables us to use two-input NNs even in high-dimensional datasets and capture feature interactions. We demonstrate that the proposed models are computationally efficient compared to vanilla NAM and NBM, and they exhibit better or comparable performance with state-of-the-art GAMs.

</details>


### [310] [Physics-Informed Neural Network with Squeeze-Excitation-like Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19853)
*Yun-Fei Song,Long-Gang Pang,Fu-Peng Li,Jun-Jie Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SEA-PINN is a physics-informed neural network with Squeeze-Excitation attention, showing reduced initialization loss, high stability, and competitive accuracy across diverse tasks, especially boosting high-frequency problem models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the robustness, efficiency, and nonlinear representation power of physics-informed neural networks while addressing challenges in optimization and high-frequency problem solving.

Method: Incorporates a Squeeze-Excitation-like attention mechanism into PINNs for dynamic neuron recalibration across layers and employs stable initialization for optimization enhancement.

Result: SEA-PINN demonstrated negligible variance, reduced initial loss, and competitive accuracy on 17 of 20 benchmarks, with substantial performance improvements in high-frequency problem solving, especially when integrated with TSA-PINN.

Conclusion: SEA-PINN is an effective, lightweight plug-in module for enhancing PINN architectures, improving reliability and convergence while efficiently addressing nonlinear and high-frequency learning challenges.

Abstract: We introduce SEA-PINN, a novel architecture that incorporates a Squeeze-Excitation-like attention mechanism into physics-informed neural networks to dynamically recalibrate the importance of neurons across layers. A key feature of SEA-PINN is its highly stable initialization. On 17 out of 20 benchmark problems, SEA-PINN exhibit nearly negligible variance and significantly reduced initial loss, establishing a quasi-deterministic and favorable starting point for optimization. Notably, without employing Fourier feature embeddings or periodic activation functions, SEA-PINN attained competitive accuracy (83\% vs. 90\% improvement relative to FNN-PINN on the high-frequency case 7) as compared with TSA-PINN-a model specifically engineered for high-frequency problems via learnable frequencies in sinusoidal activations. Furthermore, integrating SEA-PINN into TSA-PINN boosted performance by 42.49\%. These results underscore SEA-PINN as a lightweight plug-in module that enhances nonlinear representation power, promotes more robust and efficient convergence, and strengthens the overall reliability of physics-informed learning.

</details>


### [311] [Global Convergence of Gradient Descent for Score Matching in Gaussian Mixtures via Reverse Fisher Divergence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19876)
*Alexander Tyurin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper investigates using reverse Fisher divergence over forward Fisher divergence for score matching objectives, demonstrating better optimization properties and global convergence guarantees for Gaussian mixture models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The traditional forward Fisher divergence method in score matching can lead to poor optimization and convergence behavior, particularly in Gaussian mixture model settings.

Method: The authors analyze gradient descent on Gaussian mixture models by switching to the reverse Fisher divergence, using a Lyapunov-based approach to study optimization landscapes and dynamics.

Result: They prove global convergence for gradient descent in both single Gaussian teachers and Gaussian mixture model cases, contingent on initialization and separation assumptions, with conditions for convergence in total variation distance.

Conclusion: Reverse Fisher divergence offers a more favorable optimization landscape and robust global convergence properties compared to forward Fisher divergence, making it a promising alternative for score matching tasks.

Abstract: The score matching problem is a central training objective in modern generative modeling, diffusion models, fitting unnormalized statistical models, and inverse problems. A standard approach is to minimize the forward Fisher divergence, where the expectation is taken with respect to the teacher distribution. However, recent results show that even in simple Gaussian mixture model settings, this objective can lead to undesirable and initialization-dependent convergence behavior. In this paper, we study an alternative objective: the reverse Fisher divergence, where the expectation is taken with respect to the student distribution. We analyze gradient descent (GD) for fitting Gaussian mixture models and show that this change in the objective leads to significantly better optimization properties. First, when the teacher distribution is a single Gaussian and the student is a Gaussian mixture model with fixed weights and identity covariances, we prove the global convergence of GD from arbitrary initializations. Second, we extend the analysis to the case where the teacher is also a Gaussian mixture model and prove global convergence guarantees under a global random initialization scheme and a $\widetildeΩ(1)$-separation assumption on the target means. In particular, with high probability, each student component converges near its closest teacher component, and we provide conditions under which the student distribution converges in total variation distance. Our proofs rely on a new Lyapunov-based analysis of the gradient descent dynamics, showing that the reverse Fisher divergence has a much more favorable optimization landscape than the forward Fisher divergence.

</details>


### [312] [SL-S4Wave: Self-Supervised Learning of Physiological Waveforms with Structured State Space Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19888)
*Feng Wu,Harsh Deep,Eric Lehman,Sanyam Kapoor,Guoshuai Zhao,Rahul Krishnan,Gari Clifford,Li-wei H Lehman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents SL-S4Wave, a self-supervised learning framework designed to model long-sequence medical time series data like ECGs and EEGs, addressing challenges such as noise, multichannel complexity, and long-range dependencies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Capturing long-range dependencies and noise-invariant features in multichannel medical waveforms has been difficult for existing methods like convolutional architectures or standard structured state space models.

Method: SL-S4Wave combines contrastive self-supervised learning with a novel structured state space model (S4) encoder capable of multiscale convolution to capture both local and long-range patterns in noisy multichannel waveforms.

Result: SL-S4Wave achieves state-of-the-art performance in arrhythmia detection tasks, excels with fewer labeled data, robustly handles long sequences, generalizes to unseen arrhythmia types, and performs well on EEG tasks.

Conclusion: SL-S4Wave effectively models complex, noisy physiological waveforms across domains, offering a generalizable and label-efficient solution for both cardiac and EEG time series data.

Abstract: Modeling long-sequence medical time series data, such as electrocardiograms (ECG), poses significant challenges due to high sampling rates, multichannel signal complexity, inherent noise, and limited labeled data. While recent self-supervised learning (SSL) methods, based on various encoder architectures such as convolutional neural networks, have been proposed to learn representations from unlabeled data, they often fall short in capturing long-range dependencies and noise-invariant features. Structured state space models (S4) excel at long-sequence modeling, but existing S4 architectures fail to capture the unique characteristics of multichannel physiological waveforms. In this work, we propose SL-S4Wave, a self-supervised learning framework that combines contrastive learning with a tailored encoder built on structured state space models. The encoder incorporates multi-layer global convolution using multiscale subkernels, enabling the capture of both fine-grained local patterns and long-range temporal dependencies in noisy, high-resolution multichannel waveforms. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that SL-S4Wave (1) consistently outperforms state-of-the-art supervised and self-supervised baselines in a challenging arrhythmia detection task, (2) achieves high performance with significantly fewer labeled examples, showcasing strong label efficiency, and (3) maintains robust performance on long waveform segments, highlighting its capacity to model complex temporal dynamics in long sequences that most existing approaches fail to efficiently model, and (4) transfers effectively to unseen arrhythmia types, underscoring its robust cross-domain generalization. We additionally evaluate SL-S4Wave on multiple EEG tasks, achieving superior performance over strong baselines, demonstrating generalizability of our approach beyond cardiac waveforms.

</details>


### [313] [Adversarial Bandit Optimization with Globally Bounded Perturbations to Convex Losses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19891)
*Zhuoyu Cheng,Kohei Hatano,Eiji Takimoto*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper analyzes adversarial bandit optimization with loss functions that include both convex and $β$-smooth components and adversarial perturbations subject to a global budget.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of extending globally budgeted perturbation models to non-linear loss functions while quantifying their impact on regret.

Method: The paper modifies a standard bandit optimization algorithm and develops a novel regret analysis to account for the perturbation budget.

Result: Expected regret bounds are established, explicitly characterizing the impact of perturbation budgets on performance.

Conclusion: The framework successfully extends existing models to a broader range of loss functions and provides regret guarantees that match the standard bandit optimization in the absence of perturbations.

Abstract: We study adversarial bandit optimization in which the loss functions may be non-convex and non-smooth. In each round, the learner selects an action and observes only the loss incurred at that action. The loss consists of an underlying convex and $β$-smooth component and an adversarial perturbation that may be chosen after observing the learner's action. The perturbations are subject to a global budget controlling their cumulative magnitude over time.
  This framework extends the globally budgeted, post-action perturbation model from underlying linear losses to general convex and $β$-smooth losses. For this broader class, we establish expected regret guarantees that explicitly characterize the effect of the perturbation budget.
  To establish these guarantees, we modify a standard bandit optimization algorithm and develop an analysis that controls the additional regret caused by the perturbations. In the absence of perturbations, our results reduce to regret guarantees for the standard bandit convex optimization setting with $β$-smooth losses.

</details>


### [314] [Score Approximation for Diffusion Models on Arbitrary Low-Dimensional Structures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19894)
*Xinhe Mu,Zaijiu Shang,Zhaoqi Zhou,Chuan Zhou,Qi Meng,Guiying Yan,Zhiming Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a universal theorem for score approximation in diffusion models, overcoming challenges posed by irregular and non-smooth data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the theoretical limitations of diffusion models when applied to real-world data with non-smooth structures, sharp boundaries, and disjoint clusters.

Method: A novel discrete-mixture approach to establish a universal theorem using ReLU networks for efficient score approximation tied to upper Minkowski dimension theory.

Result: Score functions can be approximated without the exponential dependency on ambient dimension but only on the upper Minkowski dimension $d$.

Conclusion: Diffusion models can adapt effectively to complex, irregular data distributions, enabling their robust performance for real-world generative applications.

Abstract: The remarkable success of score-based diffusion models has spurred significant efforts to establish their theoretical foundations. However, existing complexity bounds for score approximation rely heavily on restrictive assumptions like Lipschitz continuous densities or smooth manifold supports, which are routinely violated by the singularities, sharp boundaries, and disjoint clusters inherent to real-world perceptual data. This work establishes a universal score approximation theorem that works for any distribution supported on any compact set of upper Minkowski dimension $d$. Using a novel discrete-mixture formulation, we prove that the score function can be approximated with a ReLU network whose complexity grows exponentially only with $d$, thus breaking the exponential curse of ambient dimensionality. Combined with existing theories on accurately solving the backward diffusion SDE for arbitrary compact distributions, our work shows that diffusion models readily adapt to irregular, non-smooth data structures, explaining their competence in real-world generative tasks.

</details>


### [315] [ADaPT: Token-Level Decoupling for Efficient Large Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19919)
*Tingyun Li,Zishang Jiang,Jinyi Han,Xinyi Wang,Sihang Jiang,Han Xia,Zhaoqian Dai,Shuguang Ma,Fei Yu,Jiaqing Liang,Yanghua Xiao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces ADaPT, a token-level framework for achieving efficient reasoning without sacrificing accuracy by decoupling efficiency and correctness signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the computational inefficiencies and accuracy trade-offs faced by large reasoning models relying on lengthy reasoning chains.

Method: They propose Adaptive Dual-Process Thinking (ADaPT), which uses a mode-selection token to selectively apply efficiency rewards and manage fast and slow reasoning during training and inference.

Result: ADaPT demonstrated reduced computational costs while maintaining reasoning accuracy across various benchmarks.

Conclusion: ADaPT offers an effective solution for balancing efficiency and reasoning performance, with precise control over the trade-off at inference time.

Abstract: Large reasoning models rely on long chain-of-thought to achieve strong performance, but applying such reasoning uniformly incurs high computational cost. Existing efficiency-oriented methods attempt to shorten or mix reasoning strategies, yet often degrade reasoning capability. We identify the root cause as sequence-level coupling between efficiency incentives and correctness optimization, which implicitly penalizes long but correct reasoning trajectories. To address this issue, we propose Adaptive Dual-Process Thinking (ADaPT), a token-level dual-process framework that explicitly decouples efficiency and correctness signals during training. ADaPT introduces a mode-selection token to control fast and slow reasoning, applying efficiency-related rewards exclusively to this token to avoid penalizing correct long reasoning while encouraging efficiency when appropriate. Moreover, ADaPT enables precise and continuous control over the efficiency-performance trade-off at inference time: by adjusting the generation probability of the mode-selection token, a single trained model can smoothly move along the efficiency-performance Pareto frontier. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ADaPT significantly reduces inference cost while maintaining strong reasoning performance across multiple benchmarks.

</details>


### [316] [Compositionality Emerges in a Narrow Depth-Connectivity Regime: Architecture Constraints and Solution Manifolds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19941)
*Dat H. Do,Rushi Shah,Duc V. Le,Dianbo Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates the factors that lead to compositionality, a foundation for model generalization, demonstrating that it emerges within a narrow range of network connectivity and depth.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to understand why standard gradient-based optimization rarely leads to compositional internal structures and to identify the conditions under which compositionality arises.

Method: The authors examine the effects of network sparsity and depth, introducing similarity-based pruning (SP) and a heuristic depth predictor to isolate compositional connectivity and determine optimal depth.

Result: Compositionality is shown to emerge only in specific sparse networks and at target-dependent depths, with theoretical explanations provided based on compositional sparsity and feature-interference bounds.

Conclusion: The study highlights that compositional solutions arise within a specific depth-connectivity regime, paving the way for improved model generalization strategies.

Abstract: Compositionality is believed to be the foundation for generalization, enabling models to reuse meaningful primitives in novel combinations. Yet, models trained with standard gradient-based optimization rarely, and often only weakly, exhibit compositional internal structure, and it remains unclear how or why such compositionality forms. In this work, we show that compositionality emerges in a narrow connectivity-depth sweet spot. Along the connectivity axis, compositionality only appears in some specifically sparse networks, heavily depends on which connections remain rather than on weights' sparsity alone. Along the depth axis, compositionality emerges within a narrow, target-dependent regime, peaking at specific depths, while both shallower and deeper networks fail. When either the depth or connectivity condition is violated, gradient descent silently converges to fractured solutions rather than compositional ones. To discover and exploit this emergence, we introduce (i) similarity-based pruning (SP) to recover compositional connectivity and (ii) a heuristic depth predictor to estimate where compositionality is most likely to appear. Finally, we support these empirical findings with a theoretical framework based on compositional sparsity, volume-ratio arguments, and feature-interference bounds, explaining why compositional solutions are reachable only in a narrow depth-connectivity regime.

</details>


### [317] [Towards Graph-Based Deep Learning for Map Generalization: Insights from Building Footprints Simplification and Aggregation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19956)
*Yanning Wang,Zhiyong Zhou,Zhouyu Liu,Mengni Yu,Yu Feng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper explores how graph-based deep learning can address map generalization tasks like building-footprint simplification and aggregation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve map generalization methodologies by leveraging advanced deep learning techniques in graph-based frameworks.

Method: Graph generalization tasks are reformulated using graph neural networks (GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE) for node movement (simplification) prediction and link prediction (aggregation).

Result: GraphSAGE excels in link prediction but struggles, along with other models, in precise node movement; aggregation is shown to be more complex than simplification.

Conclusion: While graph-based deep learning offers promising insights for map generalization, challenges such as spatial complexity, data imbalance, and refinement necessities must be addressed.

Abstract: Map generalization remains one of the fundamental tasks in cartography, especially for the simplification and aggregation of complex building footprints. This study presents the first exploratory application of graph-based deep learning to both tasks, reformulating simplification as node movement prediction and aggregation as link prediction within a unified graph learning framework. We evaluate representative graph neural network architectures (GCN, GAT, and GraphSAGE) on multi-scale building datasets, showing that GraphSAGE demonstrates relative strengths in link prediction accuracy, while also revealing persistent challenges in precise node movement prediction. Beyond quantitative performance, the results highlight that aggregation poses greater complexity and challenges than simplification, underscoring the difficulty of capturing higher-level spatial relationships in map generalization with current deep learning approaches. Although limitations such as data imbalance and the need for post-processing remain, the study provides valuable insights and methodological directions for advancing automated map generalization with deep learning approaches.

</details>


### [318] [The Token Is a Group Element: On Lie-Algebra Attention over Matrix Lie Groups](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20547)
*Przemyslaw Musialski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Lie-Algebra Attention, a novel attention mechanism based on matrix Lie group tokens whose scores are algebra norms of relative poses, achieving invariant and equivariant properties with reduced computational complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
