*Shiguo Lian,Kai Wang,Zhaoxiang Liu,Wen Liu,Minjie Hua,Yutong Liu,Jiangze Yan,Xin Wang,Cong Wang,Yilin Zhang,Yi Shen,Jieyun Huang,Fang Zhao,Huanlin Gao,Ping Chen,Xinyu Yang,Kaikai Zhao,Yao Zhao,Xinggang Wang,Huishuai Zhang,Dongyan Zhao,Junping Du,Tao Chen,Xiang Gao,Qinghuai Ma*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces a four-layer architecture to optimize large model inference, focusing on making it scalable, cost-effective, and stable for real-world applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in scalability, cost-efficiency, and stability of large model services by optimizing token-oriented inference processes.

Method: A four-layer architecture: Multi-model Fusion, Model Optimization, Compute-Model Fusion, and Compute-Network-Model Fusion, is analyzed along with its technologies and industry applications.

Result: The architecture provides practical methodologies to reduce token production costs, enhance service efficiency, and ensure reliable token supply.

Conclusion: The study offers a roadmap for transitioning large model services to a more operational and cost-effective state.

Abstract: Large model inference optimization serves as a key foundation for supporting the scalable, low-cost, and highly stable operation of large model services. Centered on token-oriented inference optimization technology, this paper proposes for the first time a four-layer technical architecture consisting of Multi-model Fusion, Model Optimization, Compute-Model Fusion, and Compute-Network-Model Fusion. It systematically reviews the key technologies and current industry status across these four levels and analyzes the application value of related technologies in real-world business scenarios. This paper provides a practical technical path for reducing token production costs, improving token service efficiency, ensuring the stability of token supply, and driving the transition of large model services from being merely callable to being operable.

</details>


### [466] [A Model-Driven Approach for Developing Families of Reinforcement Learning Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20324)
*Xiaoran Liu,Istvan David*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper proposes a model-driven, hybrid genetic algorithm approach to automate the creation of reinforcement learning (RL) training environment families, demonstrated on a wildfire mitigation scenario and curriculum learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to simplify and scale the development of RL training environment families, which are manually created but essential for realistic RL problems requiring diverse yet similar environments.

Method: A hybrid genetic algorithm combining population-based global search and heuristic local search is used. Model transformations and constraints guide the search, using a model transformation engine to generate environment families.

Result: The proposed approach was validated successfully in a wildfire mitigation scenario and curriculum learning setup, showcasing its soundness and efficiency.

Conclusion: The method offers a scalable and automated process for generating families of RL training environments, reducing manual effort and potential errors while addressing practical RL training needs.

Abstract: Virtual training environments are software-intensive systems in which reinforcement learning (RL) agents learn, adapt, and demonstrate meaningful behavior. Virtual training environments offer a safe and cost-efficient alternative to training agents in real-world settings. However, to converge, most realistic RL problems require training in multiple, mostly similar but slightly different environments - i.e., families of environment variants. The typical development process of environment families is a labor-intensive and error-prone manual endeavor that does not scale well. To alleviate these issues, in this paper, we propose a model-driven approach for developing families of RL training environments. To obtain the family of environments, we develop an approach and prototype tool. In our approach, a hybrid genetic algorithm - a combination of population-based global search and heuristic local search - generates environment families. Mutations and constraints are expressed as model transformations and are operationalized into a search process by a state-of-the-art model transformation engine. We demonstrate the soundness of our approach in a wildfire mitigation scenario and curriculum learning - a particular learning paradigm that relies on environment families.

</details>


### [467] [AutoPass: Evidence-Guided LLM Agents for Compiler Performance Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20373)
*Zepeng Li,Jie Ren,Zhanyong Tang,Jie Zheng,Zheng Wang*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper introduces AutoPass, a multi-agent framework leveraging LLMs for improved compiler performance tuning, yielding measurable speedups over traditional compiler optimizations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Applying LLMs to runtime performance tuning is challenging due to the complexity of microarchitectural effects and runtime noise. The authors aim to address these limitations by combining LLM capabilities with detailed compiler and runtime evidence.

Method: AutoPass integrates LLMs with the LLVM compiler, enabling intelligent optimization by utilizing the internal compiler state, intermediate representation analysis, and runtime-feedback-based iterative refinement. It operates without the need for offline training or fine-tuning.

Result: The system achieves notable geometric-mean speedups over LLVM's -O3 optimization level on both server-grade x86-64 (1.043x) and embedded ARM64 (1.117x) architectures.

Conclusion: AutoPass demonstrates significant efficiency improvements in compiler performance tuning by leveraging LLMs in a novel, training-free setup, making it versatile and applicable across multiple benchmarks and platforms.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) show promise for code compilation tasks, but applying them to runtime performance tuning is difficult due to complex microarchitectural effects and noisy runtime measurements. We present AutoPass, a multi-agent framework for compiler performance tuning that uses compiler and runtime evidence to guide LLM-generated optimization decisions. Rather than treating the compiler as a black box like prior auto-tuning schemes, AutoPass opens up the compiler to the LLM, enabling it to query compiler-internal optimization states and analyze the intermediate representation to orchestrate compiler options. The search process iteratively refines optimization configurations using measured runtime feedback to diagnose regressions and guide latency-improving edits. AutoPass operates in an inference-only, training-free setting and requires no offline training or task-specific fine-tuning, making it readily applicable to new benchmarks and platforms. We implement AutoPass on the LLVM compiler and evaluate it on server-grade x86-64 and embedded ARM64 systems. AutoPass outperforms expert-tuned heuristics and classical autotuning methods, achieving geometric-mean speedups of 1.043x and 1.117x over LLVM -O3 on x86-64 and ARM64, respectively.

</details>


### [468] [Probe-and-Refine Tuning of Repository Guidance for Coding Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20512)
*Asa Shepard,Jeannie Albrecht*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: Guidance produced via the probe-and-refine tuning procedure improves coding agent performance by enhancing repository knowledge, evidenced by a 33.0% resolution rate compared to alternatives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Determine whether instructions in AGENTS.md files improve LLM-based coding agent performance, and assess how to optimize them.

Method: Introduced probe-and-refine tuning—a process that iteratively updates repository guidance using synthetic bug-fix probes and single-shot LLM calls without agent loops.

Result: Using this method, refined files achieved a 33.0% resolve rate, outperforming static guidance files (28.3%) and unguided processes (25.5%). Improvement derived from better guidance coverage, not precision.

Conclusion: Optimized guidance files via probe-and-refine tuning enable coding agents to better navigate repositories, increasing their effectiveness without altering precision.

Abstract: LLM-based coding agents need higher-level operational knowledge about a repository (which files house which subsystems, how to run the test suite, which workflows have historically led to wrong fixes) that does not exist in the code itself. Engineers typically maintain \texttt{AGENTS.md} files to supply this context as instructions for coding agents, but whether they help is contested: recent studies disagree on whether LLM-generated guidance improves or harms agent performance. In this paper we show that how the guidance is produced is the decisive variable, and introduce \emph{probe-and-refine tuning}: a procedure that uses synthetic bug-fix probes to iteratively diagnose and patch a repository's guidance file through single-shot LLM calls, with no agent loop or tool use during tuning. On SWE-bench Verified across four independent trials with Qwen3.5-35B-A3B at 200 steps, probe-and-refine achieves 33.0\,\% mean resolve rate vs.\ 28.3\,\% for the static knowledge base used to initialize it and 25.5\,\% for an unguided baseline ($p < 0.001$ for both probe-and-refine contrasts). The improvement comes from coverage rather than precision: refined guidance produces evaluable patches for 14.5 percentage points (pp) more instances while per-patch precision remains statistically constant ($\sim$59\,\%, $p = 0.119$), showing that improved guidance helps agents reach the correct file rather than improving the quality of the changes they make. Further, a step-budget experiment shows that guidance is what lets the agent use a larger step budget productively, and a cross-model experiment with NVIDIA-Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B finds that the tuning loop degrades when the model cannot generate sufficiently diagnostic output, though per-patch precision remains constant even then.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.NC'></div>

# q-bio.NC [[Back]](#toc)

### [469] [Robust probabilistic measurement of structural-functional module consistency in infant brain development](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19739)
*Lingbin Bian,Feihong Liu,Qian Wang,Han Zhang,Dinggang Shen,the UNC/UMN Baby Connectome Project Consortium*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: This paper introduces a novel stochastic module-based approach to analyze structural-functional module consistency (SFMC) in brain networks, revealing developmental trends and variability from infancy to early childhood.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation behind this study is to better understand the structural-functional interactions in brain networks, particularly over developmental stages, by addressing inconsistencies and limitations in prior group-level neuroimaging analyses.

Method: The method involves defining stochastic modules to represent probabilistic assignments of brain regions to group-level sub-networks, allowing robust analysis of SFMC while considering inter-individual variability and uneven module sizes.

Result: Using data from the Baby Connectome Project, the study finds that SFMC decreases from 0 to 5 years old, with stronger coupling found in primary regions like visual areas and weaker coupling in advanced cognitive regions such as attention and default mode networks.

Conclusion: The stochastic module framework is useful for robustly analyzing variability and developmental differences in brain network structure-function coupling, offering insights into early cognitive and neurological development.

Abstract: Brain network is commonly divided into modules for analyzing their functionally segregated roles for group-level analysis in neuroimaging studies. Here, we introduce stochastic modules within brain networks for a robust probabilistic measurement of structural-functional module consistency (SFMC) in a group of subjects. Specifically, a stochastic module can be regarded as the chance of a brain region across subjects potentially being assigned to a group-level sub-network, characterized as an assignment probability for this brain region. This novel method has two advantages for evaluating inhomogeneous modules in brain networks. The first is that it can robustly evaluate the consistency between brain structural and functional modules whose population sizes are not necessary the same, and the second is that it is able to take into account the inter-individual variability of the modules for the groups. Moreover, compared with the conventional structural-functional coupling approach, our stochastic module-based method reveals a more pronounced decline in the coupling between structure and function, indicating stronger developmental reorganization. Our results using the dataset from Baby Connectome Project (BCP) show that the SFMC decreases from 0 to 5 years old, and is greater in primary brain regions, such as visual areas, while lower in more advanced cognitive regions, including those related to attention, control, and default mode network.

</details>


<div id='stat.ML'></div>

# stat.ML [[Back]](#toc)

### [470] [The Representational Limit of Scalar Interactions: An Interventional Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19410)
*Potito Aghilar,Sabino Roccotelli,Stanislao Fidanza,Vito Walter Anelli,Sebastiano Stramaglia,Tommaso Di Noia*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper highlights how conventional pairwise interaction scores fail to correctly disentangle uniqueness (U), redundancy (R), and synergy (S). It proposes a novel method, Stochastic Hi-Fi, for estimating U/R/S profiles effectively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the issue of conflated interaction scores, which fail to accurately reflect the roles of uniqueness, redundancy, and synergy in feature interactions.

Method: The authors introduce Stochastic Hi-Fi, a method for post-hoc predictability decomposition. It relies on interventional masked inference and includes techniques such as coupled diamond sampling and finite-sample Monte Carlo bounds to ensure robust estimates.

Result: Experiments show that Stochastic Hi-Fi improves feature interaction characterization, achieving better recovery of interaction magnitudes and outperforming baselines like GradCAM when applied to datasets including tabular SCMs, GPT-2 circuit understanding, and medical imaging data.

Conclusion: Stochastic Hi-Fi effectively disentangles U/R/S mechanisms, providing a richer understanding of feature interactions without retraining while outperforming traditional scalar interaction scoring methods.

Abstract: Signed pairwise interaction scores fundamentally conflate uniqueness (U), redundancy (R), and synergy (S). We prove this on a minimal 3-way XOR structural causal model: faithful indices such as Shapley-Taylor return zero per pair, whereas projective indices such as Shapley Interaction spread the third-order effect into pair scalars that conflate the three mechanisms. We introduce Stochastic Hi-Fi, a post-hoc, retraining-free predictability decomposition that estimates per-feature U/R/S profiles by interventional masked inference. The estimator provides exact interventional semantics, finite-sample Monte Carlo bounds, strict variance reduction from coupled diamond sampling, and uniform finite-vocabulary convergence. Across tabular SCMs, Stochastic Hi-Fi recovers structure missed by scalar baselines (up to 411x larger interaction-magnitude recovery ratios). It also separates redundant and synergistic heads in the GPT-2 IOI circuit. On NIH ChestX-ray14, Stochastic Hi-Fi matches GradCAM on Pointing Game and improves substantially on Deletion AUC.

</details>


### [471] [A Solver-Free Training Method for Predict-then-Optimize](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19587)
*Beichen Wan,Mo Liu*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper proposes a scalable method for training machine learning models for optimization tasks, using a new surrogate loss function that avoids computationally expensive solver calls, achieving faster training and competitive results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve scalability and efficiency in the training of prediction models for optimization tasks, where existing methods are computationally expensive and suffer from gradient evaluation challenges.

Method: The authors introduce a measure transformation principle to propose a surrogate loss function that eliminates the need for optimization solvers during training while maintaining theoretical guarantees like Fisher consistency and excess risk bounds.

Result: The proposed method reduces training time significantly while producing decision-making quality that matches state-of-the-art approaches in predict-then-optimize scenarios.

Conclusion: The approach is both efficient and effective, providing competitive decision quality in optimization tasks while dramatically decreasing training time compared to traditional methods.

Abstract: We propose a scalable method for training prediction (machine learning) models in the predict-then-optimize paradigm, where model outputs serve as coefficients for a subsequent linear optimization task. Directly minimizing the empirical decision regret is intractable for linear programming and combinatorial optimization since the decision mapping is piecewise constant, and the gradients are zero almost everywhere. While existing methods address this by smoothing the differentiation process, they suffer from scalability issues, since a computationally expensive solver call is required for every gradient evaluation. To address this, we propose a decision-focused learning pipeline based on a measure transformation principle, which yields a new surrogate loss that is completely optimization-solver-free during training. We establish theoretical guarantees, including Fisher consistency and excess risk bounds. Empirically, our method achieves decision quality competitive with state-of-the-art methods while reducing training time by orders of magnitude.

</details>


### [472] [Variational Consensus Monte Carlo for Bayesian Mixture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19643)
*Julie Fendler,Francesca L. Crowe,Tom Marshall,Sylvia Richardson,Paul D. W. Kirk*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a federated learning framework for Bayesian mixture models using Consensus Monte Carlo (CMC) to ensure privacy and sensitivities when sharing health data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address privacy and sharing limitations in sensitive health data while still enabling robust statistical inference.

Method: CMC approach where independent MCMC runs in data silos generate local posteriors, which are aggregated using extended variational CMC to handle non-conjugate Bayesian mixture models and cluster-matching for federated setups.

Result: Simulation studies show improved accuracy for small cluster identification compared to standard MCMC, and the framework successfully identifies multi-morbidity patterns in large-scale health data.

Conclusion: The proposed federated CMC framework provides a practical solution for privacy-preserving Bayesian mixture model inference with benefits for clustering accuracy and applicability in health data analysis.

Abstract: Motivated by the privacy, sensitivity and sharing limitations of health data, we present a comprehensive pipeline for inference of Bayesian mixture models within a federated learning setting, i.e. when data cannot be fully shared or pooled across compute nodes. We adopt a Consensus Monte Carlo (CMC) approach, in which an MCMC algorithm is run independently within each data silo to estimate local posterior distributions, which are then aggregated to approximate the posterior over the full data. The variational CMC approach of Rabinovich, Angelino and Jordan (2015) [1] frames the aggregation step as a variational inference problem, but their application to mixtures assumes the number of clusters and key mixture parameters to be known. Our main methodological contributions are: (i) an extension of variational CMC to over-fitted Bayesian mixture models that infer the number of clusters and all model parameters, without requiring conjugacy; (ii) novel cluster-matching algorithms suitable for cross-silo settings in which not every cluster appears in each local dataset; (iii) a number of inference strategies for the aggregation step, matched to different federated learning constraints; and (iv) guidelines for choosing among these in practice. A comprehensive simulation study validates the framework and allows us to compare to state-of-the-art federated learning alternatives. Notably, we show that when the composition of local datasets reflects the underlying clustering structure in the data, our approach can recover small clusters with greater accuracy than standard MCMC applied to the pooled data. We illustrate the framework on large-scale electronic health record data, identifying multi-morbidity patterns in a British geriatric population.

</details>


### [473] [AURA: Adaptive Uncertainty-aware Refinement for LLM-as-a-Judge Auditing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19714)
*Zilong Zhang,Yi-Ting Hung,Weiyi He,Junxi Zhang,Lei Ding,Chi-Kuang Yeh*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces AURA, a framework for refining LLM evaluations by iteratively learning human-consistency signals and focusing on uncertain comparisons.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are increasingly used as judges for open-ended generation due to scalability issues with human evaluation, but their biases make them imperfect proxies for human judgment.

Method: The authors propose AURA, which iteratively refines the evaluation process by learning human-consistency, propagating evidence, and identifying uncertain instances for human validation.

Result: The framework was tested on synthetic and real data, demonstrating its ability to improve evaluation reliability and refine trust in LLM-based judgments.

Conclusion: AURA effectively tackles the biases in LLM-based judgment by refining trust as evidence accumulates, offering a scalable and reliability-focused approach to auditing pairwise decisions.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as judges for open-ended generation, as large-scale human evaluation is often expensive and difficult to scale, yet their preferences remain imperfect proxies for human judgment. Existing auditing pipelines often assume that a reliable subset of examples or clean supervision signals are available beforehand, for example from human annotation, heuristic filtering, or the outputs of strong judges. In LLM evaluation, this assumption is fragile: the initial split may inherit judge bias, while human verification is typically too scarce to define stable groups at scale. We propose AURA, an adaptive uncertainty--aware refinement framework for auditing pairwise LLM--as--a--judge decisions under selected human verification. AURA iteratively learns a human-consistency signal, propagates reliable evidence, and prioritizes uncertain comparisons for human review. The key idea is to treat trust in a judge as a latent quantity that is progressively refined as evidence accumulates. We provide a compact formulation, a stable refinement procedure, and a comprehensive evaluation on both synthetic and real pairwise LLM-answer data.

</details>


### [474] [Stochastic Linear Contextual Bandits with Bounded Noise: A Set-Membership Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20022)
*Haonan Xu,Yingying Li*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces SME-OFU, a novel algorithm for stochastic linear contextual bandits with bounded reward noise, achieving a regret bound of $O(\log T)$ compared to the typical $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{T})$ for sub-Gaussian noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing stochastic linear contextual bandit algorithms assume sub-Gaussian reward noise, achieving suboptimal regret bounds in cases where noise and realized rewards are bounded. The authors aim to optimize performance under bounded reward noise conditions.

Method: The authors developed SME-OFU by leveraging set-membership estimation (SME) and the optimism in the face of uncertainty (OFU) principle to explicitly handle bounded reward noise.

Result: SME-OFU achieves an improved $O(\log T)$ regret bound under bounded reward noise, confirmed through simulations which show empirical improvements over a benchmark designed for sub-Gaussian noise.

Conclusion: Utilizing bounded reward noise and SME-OFU, the paper establishes a more efficient regret bound in stochastic linear contextual bandits, outperforming prior approaches when rewards are bounded.

Abstract: This paper considers stochastic linear contextual bandits (SLCB) with bounded reward noise. Existing works typically assume sub-Gaussian reward noise and bounded expected rewards, under which the optimal regret bound scales as $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{T})$ in terms of horizon $T$. However, in many applications, realized/observed rewards are also naturally bounded, implying bounded reward noise. Bounded noise is more informative than the sub-Gaussian condition but has not been leveraged explicitly in the SLCB literature. In this paper, we propose a novel algorithm SME-OFU by utilizing an uncertainty quantification method called set-membership estimation (SME) and applying the principle of optimism in the face of uncertainty (OFU). Our algorithm enjoys an improved regret bound $O(\log T)$. Notice that this does not contradict the existing optimal bound $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{T})$ for sub-Gaussian noise because bounded noise is a stronger condition. Finally, simulations show empirical improvements of SME-OFU over a benchmark algorithm designed for sub-Gaussian noise when the reward noise is bounded.

</details>


### [475] [AK-MCS-C2 : Active Kriging Monte Carlo Simulation method with conformal certification for failure probability estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20191)
*Edgar Jaber,Vincent Chabridon,Mathilde Mougeot*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper proposes an advanced framework combining Active Kriging Monte Carlo simulation with conformal prediction to improve failure probability estimation, particularly for small samples and rare events.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations in traditional methods for structural reliability analysis, particularly the lack of distribution-free guarantees and inefficient handling of small-sample and rare-event scenarios.

Method: The framework integrates Active Kriging Monte Carlo simulation with a cross-conformal strategy tailored for J+GP conformal estimators, ensuring more reliable uncertainty quantification near the limit-state surface.

Result: The method demonstrates improved accuracy and robustness in failure probability estimates compared to classical approaches, especially in rare-event regimes, as shown in numerical benchmark comparisons.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a more reliable and efficient tool for structural reliability analysis, offering better performance in uncertainty quantification and sample classification near critical thresholds.

Abstract: We introduce a novel active-learning framework for failure probability estimation in structural reliability analysis that integrates Active Kriging Monte Carlo simulation with conformal prediction. The proposed approach employs an adaptive cross-conformal strategy specifically designed for small-sample settings and kriging surrogate models using the J+GP conformal estimator. Unlike standard AK-MCS methods, the proposed framework provides distribution-free guarantees on prediction errors, leading to more reliable classification of samples near the limit-state surface. This improved uncertainty quantification enhances both the accuracy and robustness of failure probability estimates, especially for rare-event regimes where such efficiency is crucial. Reproducible numerical results illustrate the effectiveness of the method and also compare it to classical approaches on well-established benchmarks.

</details>


### [476] [Off-Policy Evaluation for Missingness-Aware Policies in MDPs with Rewards Missing Not at Random](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20206)
*Ziheng Wei,Annie Qu,Rui Miao*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper addresses off-policy evaluation in offline reinforcement learning when rewards are missing not at random (MNAR), breaking standard assumptions, and proposes methods to handle this through identification and estimation techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The problem of rewards missing not at random (MNAR) in offline reinforcement learning leads to selection bias and inaccurate evaluations, particularly relevant in high-stakes fields like health care and marketing.

Method: The authors introduce a reward-dependent propensity model, use future states as shadow variables, and incorporate a bridge function recovered through a min-max optimization procedure. They propose a Fitted-Q-Evaluation-style estimator to propagate the identified rewards.

Result: The proposed method demonstrates consistency, finite-sample error bounds, and better performance on both simulated datasets and real-world MIMIC-III Sepsis data compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: The paper provides a robust technique for addressing missing rewards in offline RL contexts, contributing to more accurate decision-making in practical applications with missing or biased data.

Abstract: In offline Reinforcement Learning, immediate rewards in logged batch data are often unobserved due to sparse or irregular record-keeping, or censored beyond certain reward values. This issue arises in practical settings, including health care and marketing. We investigate off-policy evaluation (OPE) in finite-horizon Markov decision processes when rewards are missing not at random (MNAR), which breaks ignorability and induces selection bias even after conditioning on states and actions. To address this, we formalize a reward-dependent propensity model and use future states as shadow variables to identify the full-data conditional mean reward. We further introduce a bridge function that recovers the conditional mean reward without explicitly modeling the MNAR mechanism, and estimate it via a min-max procedure to avoid double sampling. Building upon these identification results, we propose an Fitted-Q-Evaluation-style estimator that propagates the recovered rewards while allowing target policies to depend on past missingness indicators. Finally, we establish consistency and finite-sample error bounds for our OPE estimator, and show through experiments the strong performance of our method compared to existing methods on simulated and MIMIC-III Sepsis data.

</details>


### [477] [Statistical Properties of Training & Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20299)
*Itay Lavie,Noam Levi,Yonatan Kahn*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper examines deep learning from a physics-informed perspective, focusing on neural scaling laws and inductive biases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how deep learning achieves unprecedented performance in real-world tasks through a physics-informed lens.

Method: Review and discuss neural scaling laws and investigate inductive bias constraints in physics-related problems.

Result: Insights into neural scaling laws and their interplay with inductive biases in applying deep learning to physics problems.

Conclusion: Physics-informed perspectives can help justify choices in constructing deep learning models and improve their application in scientific domains.

Abstract: Deep learning has managed to evade numerous intuitions from classical statistics to achieve unprecedented performance on a number of real-world tasks. In this article, we investigate the key features and surprises of deep learning from a physics-informed perspective, taking care to point out and justify where possible the many choices inherent in constructing a deep learning model. In particular, we review the phenomenon of neural scaling laws and discuss their interplay with the constraints and inductive biases which may be present when applying machine learning to problems in physics.

</details>


### [478] [SSH-Net: A Deep Neural Network for Predicting Failure Time Distribution Functions under Competing Risks with Application to GPU Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20451)
*Jie Min,Yueyao Wang,Mengkun Chen*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper proposes a Structured Segmented Hazard Deep Neural Network (SSH-Net) for failure time prediction under competing risks, addressing challenges with hyperparameter tuning and hierarchical data structures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing challenges in time-to-event modeling include hyperparameter tuning for complex neural networks and capturing hierarchical data structures, especially in engineering applications with competing risks.

Method: The SSH-Net integrates the structure of neural networks with data hierarchies, uses separate subnetworks for covariate groups, employs a cause-specific competing risks model, and is trained with a penalized log-likelihood loss function.

Result: Through simulations and validation with Titan GPU failure data, SSH-Net demonstrates improved prediction accuracy using metrics like Brier score, AUC, and RMSE.

Conclusion: The proposed SSH-Net effectively addresses complexities in time-to-event modeling under competing risks, providing a robust method for learning from hierarchical data and improving prediction accuracy.

Abstract: Competing risks are commonly observed in engineering fields and can bring challenges to time-to-event data modeling when the application scenarios are complicated. Recently, deep neural networks have received great attention for prediction with competing risks, due to their flexibility and high learning capability. However, the complexity of neural network structure brings extra difficulty in hyperparameter tuning based on different data inputs. Additionally, when an engineered system has complex physical structures with multiple hierarchical levels, treating all structural levels as a single group of inputs may fail to capture critical information. To address the issues, we propose a Structured Segmented Hazard Deep Neural Network (SSH-Net) for failure time prediction under cause-specific competing risks framework. Our approach associates neural network structure with data structures, and allows different covariate groups to impact the failure prediction through separate sub-networks. The neural network is constructed based on a cause-specific competing risks model. The SSH-Net outputs cause-specific hazard functions, and utilizes the penalized log-likelihood as the loss function. The prediction accuracy of SSH-Net is validated through simulation studies by evaluating the Brier score, the area under receiver operating characteristic curves (AUC), and the root mean square error (RMSE) of the predicted cause-specific cumulative incident function. We further demonstrate the model's ability to predict failure time distribution functions using the Titan GPU failure time data.

</details>


<div id='quant-ph'></div>

# quant-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [479] [Quantum ring all-reduce: communication and privacy advantages for distributed learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20344)
*María Gragera Garcés,Lirandë Pira*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: This paper proposes leveraging quantum communications for distributed machine learning training with enhanced efficiency and security.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenges of communication efficiency and privacy in large-scale distributed machine learning training using innovative quantum communication techniques.

Method: The authors develop a quantum version of the Ring all-reduce communication protocol, leveraging pre-shared entanglement, superdense coding, and verified entanglement to enhance communication and security.

Result: The paper achieves a provably optimal factor of two communication efficiency improvement and information-theoretic privacy for distributed training. It also characterizes significant quantum advantages in specific gradient conflict detection tasks.

Conclusion: Quantum communication can provide simultaneous communication and privacy benefits for distributed machine learning training, whether the models are classical or quantum, showcasing practical hybrid quantum-classical advantages.

Abstract: Machine learning models have scaled to unprecedented sizes, making training across distributed devices the de facto standard in the field. In this work, we explore how quantum communications can make distributed training both more communication-efficient and information-theoretically private, for both classical and quantum learning models. Ring all-reduce is the foundational communication primitive for large-scale distributed training. We present a quantum version that reduces per-link online communication by a provably optimal factor of two using pre-shared entanglement and superdense coding, without requiring the learning model or gradient computation to change. Beyond bandwidth, the primitive enables privacy guarantees that are information-theoretically impossible for any classical protocol, achieving composable ε-secure aggregation, via verified entanglement, at a 2x overhead in GHZ copies. Our hybrid quantum-classical communication architecture yields simultaneous communication and security advantages for large scale distributed training, regardless of whether the learning itself is quantum or classical. Finally, we characterise quantum advantages in gradient conflict detection for server-to-client communication under bandwidth constraints, a setting that arises after ring all-reduce is completed, when full gradient broadcast to external clients is infeasible. Two variants of the problem admit different separations. For margin-based alignment testing (\textsc{GapIP}_τ), the quantum advantage is quadratic in the margin parameter: \widetilde{O}(τ^{-1}\log P) qubits versus \widetilde{O}(\min(\τ^{-2},P)) bits. For sign-consistency auditing against a private parameter matching (\textsc{TieAudit}_ε), the advantage represents an exponential separation in communication complexity: Ω(\sqrt{P}) bits whereas O(ε^{-2}\log P) qubits suffice.

</details>


### [480] [Optimal Ansatz-free Hamiltonian Learning In Situ](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19486)
*Taiqi Zhou,Weiyuan Gong*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: The paper introduces a computationally efficient, control-free algorithm for learning a Hamiltonian using Pauli product states and measurements, offering optimal performance for near-term quantum platforms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges posed by existing Hamiltonian learning protocols requiring deep circuits and extreme time resolution, making them impractical for near-term quantum experiments.

Method: The authors propose a randomized-sampling framework with band-limited kernel-based time sampling and a displacement sieve for ansatz-free Hamiltonian learning.

Result: The algorithm achieves optimal evolution time for control-free protocols and remains robust in the presence of SPAM noise in practical quantum setups.

Conclusion: The research sets fundamental cost benchmarks for Hamiltonian learning while enabling feasible, high-precision quantum device calibration and sensing.

Abstract: Characterizing the features of a Hamiltonian that governs a quantum system serves as a fundamental subroutine of quantum device calibration, signal sensing, and error correction. Recent works proposed protocols have achieved the optimal Heisenberg-limited scaling learning ansatz-free Hamiltonians from their real-time evolutions without fully specifying interaction structures. However, these protocols rely on both deep circuits with interleaving probes and control, and extremely short time resolution, making them difficult to implement on near- and intermediate-term in situ quantum experiments. In this work, we propose a computationally efficient, control-free, and ancilla-free algorithm that uses only Pauli product state preparation and measurement, and learns an ansatz-free Hamiltonian $H$ with $||H||\leqΛ$ in total evolution time of $Θ(\fracΛ{ε^2}\log(\fracΛε))$. The evolution time cost of our algorithm is optimal for any control-free protocols as we further prove a lower bound of $Ω(\fracΛ{ε^2}\log(\fracΛε))$. Technically, our method introduces a randomized-sampling framework that combines band-limited kernel-based time sampling with a displacement sieve for Hamiltonian structure learning. The characteristic probe time resolution depends only on $Λ$ instead of $\varepsilon$, which makes our protocol especially appealing in the high-precision regime for sensing and calibration applications. We also show that the algorithm maintains the same asymptotic total evolution time in the presence of state-preparation-and-measurement (SPAM) noise when the Hamiltonian is local after calibration. Our results demonstrate the fundamental cost of experimentally friendly Hamiltonian learning and provide a practical route to rigorous in situ characterization of near-term quantum platforms.

</details>


### [481] [QMaxCal: Path-Space Regularization for Open Quantum Control via Girsanov's Theorem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19947)
*Merijn Moody,Zier Mensch,Miranda C. N. Cheng,Peter G. Bolhuis,Max Welling*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: This paper introduces two novel regularizers for enhancing quantum control robustness against decoherence through trajectory distribution comparison with the KL divergence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop reliable quantum control mechanisms capable of mitigating the effects of environmental noise on quantum systems, which is crucial for stable quantum computation.

Method: The authors derive two regularizers, Wiener KL (KL_W) and drift-variance regularizer (R_DV), based on Girsanov's theorem to evaluate the KL divergence of trajectory distributions affected by decoherence.

Result: The proposed regularizers outperform baseline methods in improving quantum control fidelity, robustness to noise model mismatches, and minimizing infidelity rates by up to 50%, with successes demonstrated on a calibrated IBM quantum processor.

Conclusion: The developed regularizers provide a novel and effective approach for mitigating decoherence in quantum systems and exhibit superior performance across multiple benchmarks and metrics.

Abstract: Reliable quantum control in the presence of decoherence requires policies that combat the effect of environmental noise on the controlled dynamics. Open quantum systems under continuous monitoring generate classical measurement records whose drift depends on the noise experienced by the system; the records of two evolutions sharing the same decoherence channels differ only in this drift, so Girsanov's theorem yields a closed-form, differentiable estimator of the KL divergence between their trajectory distributions. We instantiate this estimator with two physically motivated reference measures, yielding two regularizers that both drive the system toward states where the effects of decoherence are minimal: the Wiener KL (KL_W), which is empirically more effective under certain conditions on the noise model, and the drift-variance regularizer (R_DV), which works for all noise models. Both are qualitatively distinct from existing penalties on control fluence or smoothness: they penalize the observable consequences of control on the decoherence channels rather than the control amplitude itself. The regularizers outperform unregularized gradient-based and reinforcement-learning baselines across a range of open quantum systems -- including single- and multi-qubit benchmarks and a multi-qubit chain calibrated to a published snapshot of the IBM Kingston processor -- along several axes of evaluation: final-state fidelity, robustness to mismatch in the assumed noise model (gains grow from +17 pp at training noise to +27 pp under 2.5x noise mismatch), and occupation of forbidden states. The regularizers reduce infidelity by up to 50%, with ~16% gains on the calibrated IBM Kingston chain.

</details>


### [482] [Entropy Estimation in Multi-Qutrit Systems via Variational and Classical Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20504)
*Sai Sakunthala Guddanti,Anil Prabhakar,Ria Rushin Joseph*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: This paper compares von Neumann entropy estimation methods for multi-qutrit systems using Variational Quantum Algorithms (VQAs) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), finding VQAs suitable for small systems and CNNs excelling in scalability and robustness for larger systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore effective methods for estimating von Neumann entropy in multi-qutrit quantum systems, ensuring accuracy and scalability as system size increases.

Method: The study employs 11 SU(3)-inspired VQA ansatzes for small systems and a CNN trained on tensor-product mutually unbiased bases for larger systems, tested on an ideal quantum simulator.

Result: VQAs show that estimation accuracy depends on trainable parameters and entanglement, while CNNs provide scalable and robust entropy predictions, needing fewer measurements and performing better for larger systems.

Conclusion: VQAs are effective for smaller quantum systems, but CNN-based estimators offer superior scalability and robustness for larger systems, making them a practical choice for entropy estimation in these contexts.

Abstract: We present a systematic study of von Neumann entropy estimation in multi-qutrit quantum systems using two complementary approaches: variational quantum algorithms (VQAs) and classical convolutional neural networks (CNNs), evaluated using an ideal (noise-free) quantum simulator. For systems up to three qutrits, we construct and evaluate 11 hardware-efficient SU(3)-inspired ansatzes. A parameter sweep shows that estimation accuracy is primarily determined by the number of trainable parameters, provided sufficient entanglement is present. Based on this study, we fix the parameter count to approximately 120 for subsequent experiments, observing that increasing entangling-gate counts beyond a threshold yields only marginal improvements. For larger systems (two to five qutrits), we use a CNN trained on measurement outcomes from tensor-product mutually unbiased bases. The model achieves accurate and stable predictions and exhibits a systematic improvement in performance with system size, with the highest errors for two-qutrit systems and the lowest for five-qutrit systems. Notably, using only 12.5% of the measurements required for full state tomography is sufficient to reach 90th-percentile absolute errors of approximately 0.13-0.16 nats for both four- and five-qutrit systems. The CNN model is also robust to shot noise and generalizes well to out-of-distribution states. Overall, within the simulated settings studied here, our results indicate a transition in practical methods: VQAs are effective for small systems, while CNN-based estimators offer improved scalability and robustness for larger qutrit systems.

</details>


<div id='physics.ao-ph'></div>

# physics.ao-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [483] [Rigorous uncertainty quantification of probabilistic AI weather forecasts with conformal prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19642)
*Anna Asch,Raphael Rossellini,Pedram Hassanzadeh,Rebecca Willett*

Main category: physics.ao-ph

TL;DR: The paper highlights the limitations of probabilistic weather forecasts from AI-driven models in calibrating extreme weather events, introducing conformal prediction methods to improve statistical coverage without sacrificing accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance uncertainty quantification in probabilistic weather forecasts and address calibration challenges in extreme events using modern AI models.

Method: The paper uses conformal prediction, a statistical methodology ensuring guaranteed coverage without assuming distributions, applied to temperature and precipitation forecasts of leading AI weather models.

Result: Implementation of conformal prediction leads to well-calibrated uncertainty in forecasts from state-of-the-art AI models like GenCast, NeuralGCM, and AIFS-ENS, including for extreme events.

Conclusion: Conformal prediction significantly improves the calibration of weather forecasts and can universally enhance any forecasting model without affecting other probabilistic accuracy measures.

Abstract: Probabilistic weather forecasting is undergoing rapid transformation with artificial intelligence (AI). In traditional numerical weather prediction, computing power can limit how well ensemble forecasts approximate the unknown statistical distribution of future states. AI models facilitate larger ensembles and are trained with probabilistic considerations, ideally leading to better uncertainty quantification. Forecasts from these state-of-the-art models are often considered well-calibrated. However, here we show that the statistical coverage of such models, the ultimate measure of calibration, can struggle, especially on extreme events. To address this shortcoming, we employ conformal prediction, a class of statistical methods that mathematically guarantees coverage under no distributional assumptions, unlike previous post-processing techniques. We apply online conformal prediction to temperature and precipitation forecasts (including extremes) of three leading global weather models, GenCast, NeuralGCM, and AIFS-ENS, ensuring calibrated uncertainty at no expense to other probabilistic metrics. This post-processing method can be applied to any forecasting model.

</details>


<div id='stat.ME'></div>

# stat.ME [[Back]](#toc)

### [484] [Overfitted high-dimensional matrix factorizations via adaptive spectral shrinkage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19540)
*Lorenzo Mauri,David B. Dunson*

Main category: stat.ME

TL;DR: The paper introduces EigenBayes, a faster Bayesian method for factor models in high-dimensional data, providing efficient and accurate uncertainty quantification and eliminating the need for computationally heavy MCMC.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Bayesian factor models are computationally expensive due to reliance on Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC), and there is a need for efficient methods to determine latent dimensions, especially in low signal-to-noise settings.

Method: The proposed EigenBayes method uses spectral estimation and adaptive empirical Bayes to calibrate hyperparameters, delivering analytically tractable posterior distributions without requiring MCMC.

Result: EigenBayes adapts to varying signal-to-noise ratios and effectively eliminates unnecessary latent components, showing strong theoretical guarantees and superior empirical performance in both simulations and a genomics case study.

Conclusion: EigenBayes is a computationally efficient, accurate alternative to traditional Bayesian factor models, suitable for high-dimensional data analyses with low signal-to-noise challenges.

Abstract: Factor models are popular approaches for analyzing high-dimensional data to extract low-rank signals and estimate covariances. They decompose the covariance matrix as the sum of low-rank and diagonal components. A key issue is how to choose the latent dimension $k$, which is particularly challenging when the factor model only holds approximately and in low signal-to-noise scenarios. Bayesian overfitted factor models specify an upper bound on $k$ and rely on structured shrinkage priors to effectively remove extra components. Such approaches are popular and effective, but computationally expensive. We propose a much faster \texttt{EigenBayes} approach that provides valid uncertainty quantification, based on spectral estimation of latent factors and adaptive empirical Bayes calibration of key hyperparameters. The resulting posterior distribution factorizes across outcomes and is analytically tractable, bypassing Markov chain Monte Carlo. We show that \texttt{EigenBayes} adapts to the signal-to-noise ratio of each outcome and latent dimension, while shrinking superfluous latent components to zero. We establish favorable asymptotic properties and demonstrate strong empirical performance in numerical experiments and a genomics application, where EigenBayes outperforms state-of-the-art alternatives.

</details>


### [485] [Machine Learning Integrated in Wavelet Shrinkage (MLShrink)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19580)
*Dixon Vimalajeewa,Vijini Lakmini,Brani Vidakovic*

Main category: stat.ME

TL;DR: This paper introduces MLShrink, a wavelet denoising method that enhances classical shrinkage by incorporating machine learning to classify ambiguous coefficients, showing better performance on complex signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical wavelet thresholding methods are often too rigid when distinguishing between signal and noise for wavelet coefficients in an intermediate range, creating a need for more flexible, data-adaptive approaches.

Method: MLShrink combines wavelet thresholding with machine learning, discarding coefficients below a lower threshold, retaining those above an upper threshold, and classifying ambiguous coefficients using local wavelet features.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows MLShrink's nonexpansive support-selection rule and that its denoising effectiveness depends on classification accuracy for ambiguous coefficients. Empirical results demonstrate its competitiveness with established methods, particularly for irregular signals.

Conclusion: MLShrink bridges classical wavelet denoising and modern machine learning, showing that learned decisions in ambiguous regions improve denoising accuracy, especially for complex signal structures.

Abstract: Data encountered in practice are frequently contaminated by additive noise, and wavelet shrinkage remains a fundamental tool for recovering underlying signals in nonparametric estimation. Classical procedures such as hard and soft thresholding decide whether to retain a wavelet coefficient almost entirely from its magnitude. Although effective in many settings, these rules can be too rigid for coefficients whose magnitudes fall in an intermediate region where the distinction between signal and noise is uncertain. We propose MLShrink, a two-threshold wavelet denoising procedure that combines wavelet shrinkage with machine learning. Coefficients below a lower threshold are discarded, coefficients above an upper threshold are retained, and coefficients in the intermediate band are classified using local wavelet-domain features. In this way, MLShrink preserves the simplicity of classical thresholding away from the decision boundary while allowing data-adaptive decisions for ambiguous coefficients. The paper also develops a theoretical framework tailored to this architecture. We show that MLShrink is a nonexpansive support-selection rule, derive an oracle-based risk decomposition showing that excess denoising risk is determined by classification errors on the undecided band, and establish an oracle-consistency result under suitable assumptions on classifier performance. Simulation experiments on standard benchmark signals indicate that MLShrink is competitive with several established wavelet shrinkage methods and is especially effective for signals with irregular, edge-rich, or non-smooth structure. These findings suggest that learned decisions on the intermediate threshold band provide a useful and interpretable connection between classical wavelet denoising and modern statistical learning.

</details>


### [486] [Calibration without labels in multiple testing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19737)
*Adway S. Wadekar,Jake A. Soloff*

Main category: stat.ME

TL;DR: The paper examines calibration of probability claims in hypothesis testing, introducing a method to assess calibration indirectly using pseudo-labels derived from $p$-values. It finds that popular measures like $q$-values can be miscalibrated.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of interpreting probabilistic claims under model misspecification in hypothesis testing, particularly when ground truths are unavailable.

Method: The authors construct pseudo-labels based on $p$-value spacings, targeting the local false discovery rate, to assess and enable post-hoc calibration in multiple testing scenarios.

Result: The study reveals that $q$-value, a prevalent measure, is often significantly miscalibrated in large-scale empirical contexts.

Conclusion: Their approach offers a novel way to assess and improve calibration in multiple testing frameworks, enhancing the reliability of probabilistic inferences even when model assumptions may fail.

Abstract: Large-scale hypothesis testing supports probability claims about individual hypotheses, as in empirical Bayes methods for estimating local false discovery rates. We study how such claims can be interpreted as approximately calibrated forecasts of the null hypothesis, yielding interpretable error probabilities even under model misspecification. Our approach draws conceptual inspiration from probabilistic forecasting but addresses a different challenge: unlike forecasting, where labels are eventually observed, in multiple testing the ground truth is never revealed, so calibration must be assessed stochastically and established indirectly. We address this challenge by constructing a set of pseudo-labels, derived from the spacings of ordered $p$-values, which have the local false discovery rate as their regression target. Our construction unlocks existing tools for assessing and performing post-hoc calibration in multiple testing. Notably, we find on a large-scale empirical survey of published psychology and neuroscience literature that the $q$-value, a popular error measure based on the false discovery rate, can be severely miscalibrated.

</details>


<div id='eess.AS'></div>

# eess.AS [[Back]](#toc)

### [487] [Investigating Human-Model Discrepancies in Speech Quality Assessment via Acoustic and Prosodic Perturbations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19951)
*Masato Takagi,Masaya Kawamura,Reo Shimizu,Yuma Shirahata*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: MOS prediction models effectively evaluate acoustic fidelity but fail to account for nuanced errors like prosody and speaker-specific attributes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To better understand the limitations of MOS prediction models in capturing quality differences beyond acoustic fidelity in TTS systems.

Method: Conducted controlled perturbations on speech samples focusing on acoustic degradation, prosody errors, and speaker-specific manipulations, and compared human MOS ratings to model predictions.

Result: Models performed well in detecting acoustic degradation but struggled with prosodic errors and showed biases towards fundamental frequency while neglecting variations in speaking rate and pitch variability.

Conclusion: MOS prediction models are insufficient for evaluating finer aspects of speech quality beyond acoustic fidelity, necessitating more sophisticated approaches to match human perceptual assessments.

Abstract: Mean opinion score (MOS) prediction models are widely used as proxy metrics in text-to-speech (TTS) research, yet their ability to capture quality differences beyond acoustic fidelity remains unclear. We investigate this via controlled perturbations on speech: acoustic degradation, prosodic errors, and manipulation of speaker-specific characteristics such as pitch and speaking rate. We obtained MOS predictions for these speech samples from both human listeners and the model, and analyzed the differences in their perceptual characteristics. Results show that most models track acoustic degradation well, while all are insensitive to prosodic errors despite large subjective score drops. For speaker characteristics, models exhibit a double dissociation: strong mean fundamental frequency (F0) biases absent in human ratings, yet insensitivity to speaking rate and F0 variability that humans notice. These findings highlight limitations of scalar MOS prediction beyond acoustic fidelity.

</details>


### [488] [PASQA: Pitch-Accent-Focused Speech Quality Assessment Model Trained on Synthetic Speech with Accent Errors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20137)
*Masaya Kawamura,Yuma Shirahata,Kentaro Mitsui,Reo Shimizu*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: The paper introduces PASQA, a model for pitch-accent-focused speech quality assessment, promising stronger alignment with human judgments by addressing localized pitch-accent errors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MOS prediction models fail to accurately address localized pitch-accent errors, leading to a need for models explicitly targeting pitch-accent correctness.

Method: The authors developed PASQA using a Japanese accent-error dataset, self-supervised learning, mora-conditioned fusion, ranking loss, an auxiliary task for error localization, and speaker-invariant training.

Result: PASQA demonstrated strong ordering accuracy for accent-error severity, especially outperforming existing models in aligning with human judgments of accent correctness.

Conclusion: PASQA successfully addresses a critical gap in MOS prediction by targeting pitch-accent errors, showing potential for improved speech quality assessment models.

Abstract: Existing mean opinion score (MOS) prediction models typically predict utterance-level naturalness MOS and can be insensitive to localized pitch-accent errors. We propose Pitch-Accent-focused Speech Quality Assessment (PASQA), which explicitly targets pitch-accent correctness. To train our model, we construct a controlled Japanese accent-error dataset by changing accent patterns using an accent-controllable text-to-speech system, and compute a pseudo accent-quality score from the accent-error rate. PASQA builds on self-supervised representations and employs mora-conditioned fusion, ranking loss, an auxiliary accent-error localization task, and speaker-invariant training. Experiments show that conventional models fail to preserve the ordering by accent-error severity, whereas PASQA achieves high ordering accuracy on both seen and unseen speakers. Further, PASQA shows stronger agreement with human accent-correctness judgments. The code is available at https://github.com/lycorp-jp/PASQA.

</details>


### [489] [Cross-Dataset, Age, and Gender Generalization: A Comprehensive Analysis of Fine-Tuning Strategies for Low-Resource Children's ASR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19791)
*Paban Sapkota,Hemant Kumar Kathania,Mikko Kurimo,Sudarsana Reddy Kadiri,Shrikanth Narayanan*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: This paper investigates improving dysarthric speech recognition using tailored acoustic features and F-TDNN, achieving significant performance gains on the TORGO database.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The variability in dysarthric speech, due to impaired articulatory precision, makes recognition difficult, motivating the need for advanced methods to improve performance.

Method: The paper explores various acoustic feature combinations for different Acoustic Models and employs F-TDNN, incorporating pitch features and optimizing training setups using the TORGO database.

Result: The methods yield a 4.65% improvement in isolated word recognition and a 4.63% improvement in sentence recognition for dysarthric speech, outperforming prior studies.

Conclusion: Carefully selected features and optimized neural network configurations enhance the state-of-the-art in dysarthric speech recognition.

Abstract: The challenge associated with recognizing dysarthric speech primarily arises from pronounced acoustic variability attributed to impaired articulatory precision. Past research has demonstrated improved recognition through the use of hybrid DNN/HMM sequence discriminative training. This paper presents a comprehensive investigation of various combinations of acoustic features tailored to different Acoustic Models, offering suitable feature selections for each. The incorporation of Pitch features notably improved recognition performance, especially for sentence recognition tasks involving dysarthric speech. Through a systematic examination of the TORGO database, we have demonstrated the potential to enhance the performance of the state-of-the-art Factorized Time Delay Neural Network (F-TDNN) model for recognizing dysarthric speech. Our methods, implemented with the F-TDNN model, resulted in a 4.65\% relative improvement in isolated word recognition and a 4.63\% relative improvement in sentence recognition for dysarthric speech, compared to previous research. This improvement effectively compensates for speech variability, attributable to our deliberate selection of the number of overlapping frames between consecutive training example chunks.

</details>


### [490] [Systematic Study of Dysarthric Speech Recognition: Spectral Features and Acoustic Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19793)
*Paban Sapkota,Hemant Kumar Kathania,Mikko Kurimo,Sudarsana Reddy Kadiri,Shrikanth Narayanan*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: This study examines ways to improve dysarthric speech recognition by optimizing acoustic features and using the F-TDNN model, achieving significant recognition performance improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of recognizing dysarthric speech, which exhibits high acoustic variability due to impaired articulatory precision.

Method: The study explored combinations of acoustic features (including Pitch features) tailored to Acoustic Models and used the F-TDNN model with optimized training parameters.

Result: The F-TDNN model demonstrated a 4.65% relative improvement in isolated word recognition and a 4.63% relative improvement in sentence recognition for dysarthric speech.

Conclusion: The deliberate selection of acoustic features and training configurations can significantly enhance dysarthric speech recognition performance, mitigating the effects of speech variability.

Abstract: The challenge associated with recognizing dysarthric speech primarily arises from pronounced acoustic variability attributed to impaired articulatory precision. Past research has demonstrated improved recognition through the use of hybrid DNN/HMM sequence discriminative training. This paper presents a comprehensive investigation of various combinations of acoustic features tailored to different Acoustic Models, offering suitable feature selections for each. The incorporation of Pitch features notably improved recognition performance, especially for sentence recognition tasks involving dysarthric speech. Through a systematic examination of the TORGO database, we have demonstrated the potential to enhance the performance of the state-of-the-art Factorized Time Delay Neural Network (F-TDNN) model for recognizing dysarthric speech. Our methods, implemented with the F-TDNN model, resulted in a 4.65\% relative improvement in isolated word recognition and a 4.63\% relative improvement in sentence recognition for dysarthric speech, compared to previous research. This improvement effectively compensates for speech variability, attributable to our deliberate selection of the number of overlapping frames between consecutive training example chunks.

</details>


### [491] [Improving End-to-End Speech Recognition for Dysarthric Speech through In-Domain Data Augmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19797)
*Paban Sapkota,Hemant Kumar Kathania,Sudarsana Reddy Kadiri,Shrikanth Narayanan*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: The paper examines speech data augmentation methodologies to enhance recognition of dysarthric speech using an end-to-end Wav2Vec2 model. Results show significant improvements in recognition accuracy using tailored augmentation approaches based on severity levels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address challenges in recognizing dysarthric speech caused by varying severity levels and the lack of sufficient data.

Method: Data augmentation methods—Speaking-Rate Modification, Pitch Modification, Formant Modification, and Vocal Tract Length Perturbation—are applied to enhance an ASR system fine-tuned for severity-specific classes using the Wav2Vec2 model.

Result: Results show improved Word Error Rates (WERs) with the best performance achieved for each severity class using specific augmentation methods: SRM for low and medium severity, and PM for high severity.

Conclusion: The tailored augmentation techniques improve dysarthric speech recognition systems, providing a pathway for severity-specific enhancements in ASR performance.

Abstract: Dysarthric speech recognition is crucial for facilitating effective communication among individuals with dysarthria. However, accurately recognizing dysarthric speech poses significant challenges due to varying severity levels and limited data availability. In this paper, we explore data augmentation techniques for dysarthric automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems by fine-tuning the End-to-End pre-trained Wav2Vec2 model, with a specific focus on severity levels. To address the challenges of data scarcity and the need for extensive data in fine-tuning pre-trained ASR systems for dysarthric speech, we investigate four prominent data augmentation methods: Speaking-Rate Modification (SRM), Pitch Modification (PM), Formant Modification (FM), and vocal tract Length Perturbation (VTLP), tailored to different aspects of dysarthria. The study uses individually fine-tuned Wav2Vec2 models for each severity class as baseline systems. Additionally, we conducted severity-specific fine-tuning of the ASR model using augmented data. Results demonstrate distinct efficacy patterns for each augmentation technique across severity levels. The best WERs were achieved with SRM ($s$=0.8) for \textit{low} (9.02\%) and \textit{medium} (38.11\%) severities, and with PM ($τ$=0.8) for \textit{high} severity (55.15\%), reflecting relative improvements of 30.02\%, 16.64\%, and 15.47\%, respectively. These results confirm the effectiveness of the augmentation methods in improving dysarthric ASR performance.

</details>


### [492] [Low-Burden Data Augmentation for Dysarthric ASR via Zero-Shot Voice Cloning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19823)
*Satwinder Singh,Qianli Wang,Zihan Zhong,Clarion Mendes,Hasegawa-Johnson,Waleed Abdulla,Seyed Reza Shahamiri*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: The study explores zero-shot voice cloning to enhance automatic speech recognition (ASR) for dysarthric speech, achieving competitive performance with reduced data collection needs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improving ASR for dysarthric speech faces challenges like limited data and variability between speakers. Traditional data augmentation methods often require speaker-specific data, which is costly to collect.

Method: The researchers used zero-shot voice cloning via Higgs Audio V2 to generate synthetic speech data from TORGO dataset speakers. They fine-tuned Whisper-medium on real, cloned, and combined datasets and evaluated their models on real held-out speech.

Result: The fine-tuned model using cloned data (Clone FT) achieved a Word Error Rate (WER) of 26.00%, comparable to those trained on real (24.44%) and combined (25.12%) data. Clone FT also performed the best (11.45% relative improvement) in cross-corpus evaluation on SAP-1102, especially for moderate-severe speakers.

Conclusion: Zero-shot voice cloning is a scalable method to generate training data, reducing the reliance on costly data collection and showing promise for improving ASR performance for dysarthric speech.

Abstract: Automatic speech recognition remains unreliable for dysarthric speech due to data scarcity and high inter-speaker variability. While synthetic data can address these gaps, traditional methods often require extensive speaker-specific data, reintroducing the collection bottleneck. We investigate zero-shot voice cloning as a low-burden augmentation strategy, using Higgs Audio V2 to clone speakers in the TORGO dataset. We fine-tune (FT) Whisper-medium on cloned, real, and hybrid data and evaluate on held-out real speech. Compared to the zero-shot (31.62%), Clone FT achieved a competitive 26.00% WER, nearly matching the 24.44% and 25.12% seen with Real and Hybrid FT, respectively. Notably, Clone and Hybrid FT outperform Real FT for moderate-severe speakers. Clone FT achieves the best results (11.45% relative) in cross-corpus evaluation on the SAP-1102. These results suggest that zero-shot cloning provides scalable training data that circumvents the costly data collection bottleneck.

</details>


### [493] [Repurposing a Speech Classifier for Guided Diffusion-Based Speech Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20457)
*Rostislav Makarov,Timo Gerkmann*

Main category: eess.AS

TL;DR: This paper explores a method of using a pretrained speech classifier for diffusion-based conditional generation, enabling speech synthesis with high quality and reduced resource usage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to simplify diffusion generation methods by repurposing pretrained discriminative models, addressing the inefficiency of requiring separate models for classification and generation tasks.

Method: The authors attach a lightweight subnetwork to a frozen noise-conditioned speech classifier and train this subnetwork using Denoising Score Matching to enable speech synthesis while reusing classifier representations.

Result: The approach demonstrates high-quality speech synthesis using a single backbone model, significantly reducing memory usage and computational cost compared to conventional methods.

Conclusion: This research bridges discriminative modeling and conditional synthesis, showing that pretrained classifiers can effectively support generation tasks, offering an efficient alternative to traditional two-model approaches.

Abstract: Classifier guidance is a way to control diffusion generation by using a noise-conditioned classifier to steer the sampling process toward a target class. One drawback of classifier guidance is that it requires two separately trained models: a classifier and a diffusion model. We therefore study a more compact alternative in which a conventionally trained speech classifier is repurposed as the backbone for diffusion generation. Starting from a frozen noise-conditioned classifier in log-Mel space, we attach a lightweight subnetwork that reuses intermediate classifier representations and train only this subnetwork under a Denoising Score Matching objective. Our work shows that a pretrained classifier can be repurposed for conditional generation, providing an appealing bridge between discriminative modeling and conditional speech synthesis resulting in high speech quality within a single-backbone model, with reduced memory footprint and computational cost.

</details>


<div id='cs.DB'></div>

# cs.DB [[Back]](#toc)

### [494] [REMOP: REmote-Memory-aware OPerator Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19576)
*Shiquan Zhang,Yunhao Mao,Yuqiu Zhang,Gengrui Zhang,Jeyhun Karimov,Hans-Arno Jacobsen*

Main category: cs.DB

TL;DR: The paper presents REMOP, a framework for optimizing out-of-memory query processing by reducing transfer rounds and improving execution under tight memory budgets, achieving significant performance gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the inefficiencies of classical operator strategies in a remote memory setting, where existing heuristics lead to excessive data-transfer rounds, impacting query runtime and latency.

Method: REMOP introduces a latency cost model that incorporates transfer rounds and derives memory policies tailored to specific operators, implementing the approach for certain operations (e.g., joins, sorts) in DuckDB.

Result: REMOP achieves up to 97% reduction in transfer rounds, up to 48% operator runtime improvement in microbenchmarks, and reduces end-to-end runtimes of TPC-H and TPC-DS queries by 22.7% and 26.4% respectively.

Conclusion: REMOP effectively mitigates remote memory latency overheads by optimizing memory policies, demonstrating substantial gains for analytical database engines under constrained memory conditions.

Abstract: Remote and disaggregated memory tiers expand the effective memory capacity of analytical database engines, but they also reshape the cost structure of out-of-memory query processing. When an operator spills beyond local DRAM, moving pages to remote memory incurs both data-transfer time and a fixed round-trip latency per transfer. Classical operator analyses and buffer-allocation heuristics primarily target disk spilling by minimizing total I/O volume. Under remote memory, these strategies can be suboptimal because they may trigger excessive transfer rounds. We present REMOP, a remote-memory-aware operator optimization framework that uses transfer-round-aware intra-operator memory policies to improve out-of-memory execution under tight memory budgets. REMOP introduces the number of transfer rounds into the latency cost model and derives operator-specific buffer-partitioning strategies, instantiating the approach for blocked nested-loop join, external merge sort, and external hash join in DuckDB. Our evaluation on a two-node compute-memory testbed shows that REMOP reduces transfer rounds by up to 97% and operator runtime by up to 48% on spill-heavy microbenchmarks, and lowers the average runtime of spilling TPC-H and TPC-DS queries by 22.7% and 26.4% end-to-end.

</details>


### [495] [The Bi-Channel Networking Paradigm for Database Systems in the Cloud](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19969)
*Georg Kreuzmayr,Muhammad El-Hindi,Benjamin Wagner,Tobias Ziegler,Viktor Leis*

Main category: cs.DB

TL;DR: The paper introduces the 'bi-channel' paradigm for database systems to address CPU overhead issues of the kernel TCP stack in fast cloud networks by co-designing database networking operations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to overcome the inefficiency caused by treating network communication as a black box with generic kernel abstractions, which leads to database performance being limited by the CPU overhead of the kernel TCP stack in fast cloud networks.

Method: The method involves introducing the 'bi-channel paradigm' that separates communication into two channels: a high-performance data path using user-space UDP for latency- and bandwidth-sensitive tasks, and a reliable control path using kernel-based TCP for coordination and recovery.

Result: The proposed paradigm achieved efficient performance, exemplified by a distributed shuffle saturating 200 Gbit/s with three CPU cores and a key-value store processing millions of messages per second.

Conclusion: Database systems should co-design networking with operations, leveraging modern NIC capabilities while maintaining TCP reliability, demonstrating significant improvements in performance and simplicity.
